chrono_text: Alalie was founded by Phokaia (no. 859), in accordance with an oracle, 20 years before the fall of Phokaia, i.e. c. 560 (Hdt. 1.165.1). After a naval battle with the Carthaginians and the Etruscans c. 540, in which the Alalians were victorious but suffered severe losses, the city was perhaps dismantled and certainly abandoned. In any case, after the battle, Alalie ceased to be a polis and its site was subsequently occupied by an Etruscan city.
pop_text: The 'epoecised' city put a fleet of sixty ships to sea (Hdt. 1.166.2), which indicates a population of c. 20,000 inhabitants (Gras (1985) 400-6) or even more (Dominguez (1985) 375-76).
relocation_details: In c. 546 the Phokaians decided to relocate their city in reaction to the Persian threat, and almost half of the Phokaians were received at Alalie. From the Alalian point of view, the arrival of these refugees may have been perceived as reception of epoikoi, while from the point of view of the Phokaians it was a case of metoikesis. After a naval battle c. 540 the city was perhaps dismantled and certainly abandoned, and the inhabitants sailed to Rhegion.

chrono_text: Emporion was founded c. 600 (Aquilué et al. (1996) 61-62), whether by Massaliote Phokaians (Ps.-Skymnos 203-4), Massaliotes (Ps.-Skylax 2; Strabo 3.4.8) or Phokaians (Livy 26.19, 34.4; cf. Pliny 3.22).
pop_text: On the basis of the size of the city (5 ha), it has been supposed that the inhabitants numbered max. 1,500-2,000 (Domínguez (1986) 4; Marcet and Sanmartí (1990) 118-19).
ha_text: On the basis of the size of the city (5 ha), it has been supposed that the inhabitants numbered max. 1,500-2,000

chrono_text: There are two different traditions about the foundation of Massalia, and consequently two different dates for the foundation. One tradition places the foundation in 600 (Timaios (FGrHist 566) fr. 7 = 120 years before the battle of Salamis), while the other places it at the time of the fall of Phokaia (no. 859) to the Persians in 546 (Antiochos (FGrHist 555) fr. 8; Isoc. Archidamus 84). The archaeological evidence supports the earlier date (Graham (2001) 38), but the existence of two different foundation dates may perhaps suggest a kind of refoundation in C6m, as a consequence of the arrival of refugees from Phokaia after the Persian conquest of Ionia.
pop_text: It has been suggested that the total population of the city never exceeded 20,000 inhabitants (Bats (1992) 273).
ha_text: During C6f Massalia slowly expanded and came to occupy almost 25 ha; in C6m the extent of the city was already more or less similar to that of the later city (c. 40 ha (Treziny (2001) 53).

chrono_text: Although the site of the Greek city has yielded ceramic evidence dating to C6l(?) and C5 (Martín, Nieto and Nolla (1979) 326-27; Vivó (1996) 112), the earliest levels associated with habitation date from C4e (Puig et al. (1994-95) 128).
There are sporadic Greek remains from C6, and substantial Greek remains from C4; the city minted a Greek-style coinage from C5m. Abakainon struck silver coins (litra, hemilitron) from c. C5m.
Adranon was founded by Dionysios I c. 400
ha_text: The impressive ashlar isodomic double curtain-wall, enclosing an area of about 60 ha, probably dates from the period of Dionysios' foundation

chrono_text: A bronze coinage of Agyrion, revealing Akragantine influence, is known from possibly as early as C5m, but at least from C5l, the period of the tyrant Agyris.
pop_text: c. 20,000 citizens (politai ouk elattons dis murion, 14.95.4); Timoleon later settled 10,000 new colonists in Agyrian territory
relocation_details: The Agyrians were granted Syracusan citizenship and the population was possibly transferred to Syracuse; Timoleon later settled 10,000 new colonists in Agyrian territory, which was presumably a regular refoundation of Agyrion.

chrono_text: In 476, Hieron transferred the populations of Katane (no. 30) and Naxos (no. 41) to Leontinoi (no. 33), renamed Katane as Aitna, and settled his new foundation with 10,000 oiketores: 5,000 from the Peloponnese and 5,000 from Syracuse. The Aitnaians were expelled from Aitna (I) in 461, retired to Inessa, a Sikel site 80 stades inland from Katane on Mt. Etna, and refounded their community there, proclaiming Hieron oikistes and preserving the name of Aitna.
pop_text: settled his new foundation with 10,000 oiketores: 5,000 from the Peloponnese and 5,000 from Syracuse
relocation_details: In 476 Hieron renamed Katane as Aitna (I). In 461 the Aitnaians were expelled from Aitna (I), retired to Inessa, and refounded their community there as Aitna (II), proclaiming Hieron oikistes and preserving the name of Aitna.

chrono_text: Akragas was founded by Gela (Thuc. 6.4.4), according to other sources with the participation of a Rhodian contingent, 108 years after the foundation of Gela, i.e. c. 580 (Thuc. 6.4.4), 100 years before the Olympic victory of Theron at the 76th Olympiad (476) according to Pindar.
pop_text: According to Diog. Laert. 8.63, Akragas had a population of 800,000 inhabitants at the time of Empedokles, an obvious exaggeration. At the time of the Carthaginian conflicts, Akragas had, according to Diod. 13.84.4, 90.3, a population of more than 20,000 citizens and with resident foreigners not less than 200,000 inhabitants.
ha_text: The urban area comprised c. 625 ha including the surrounding hills, while the habitation area on the plateau comprised c. 140 ha.
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, bouleuterion, ekklesiastierion, theatre, law_courts, stoa

chrono_text: Akrai was founded by Syracuse (no. 47) in 664 (Thuc. 6.5.2) c. 36 km west of Syracuse itself
ha_text: The urban site occupied the central part of the plateau, covering about 35 ha (Bernabo Brea (1956)).

chrono_text: According to Diod. 14. 16. 2, Alaisa was founded by Archonides of Herbita in 403/2, in order to house veteran mercenaries
No source calls Alontion a polis, but it is included here on account of its C4e coins.
Diodorus reports that Apollonia, which with Engyon (no. 14) had been ruled by the tyrant Leptines, had its autonomia restored by Timoleon in 342/1
At Troina, the archaeological remains of a circuit wall and the evidence from tombs do not antedate C4m; there is no evidence of an original Sikel settlement.

Strabo 6.2.6 discusses its foundation together with the foundations of Himera (no. 24), Kallipolis (no. 27) and Selinous (no. 44), which may suggest a C7 date for the foundation; however, Ps.-Skymnos 287-88 treats the foundation alongside that of Mylai, so a C8l date cannot be entirely excluded. Euboia's existence as a polis ended when c. 485-483 Gelon sold its demos into slavery and relocated the pacheis to Syracuse
relocation_details: c. 485-483 Gelon sold its demos into slavery and relocated the pacheis to Syracuse (Hdt. 7.156.3); after that it was a Syracusan phrourion (Strabo 10.1.15). Gelon subjected it to the same treatment as Megara.

chrono_text: Galeria is called a polis in the urban and political senses at Diod. 16.67.3 (r 345/4); politai are mentioned at Diod. 19.104.1 (r 312). The internal collective use of the city-ethnic is found on C5m coins.
Gela was founded 45 years after Syracuse, i.e. in 689/8, by colonists from Rhodos and Crete (Thuc. 6.4.3)
ha_text: The size of the Classical city, including a part of the lowland plains to the north but excluding the western cemetery and including the eastern sanctuary, was about 200 ha.

chrono_text: Archaeological evidence points to a foundation in C8l-C7e (Voza (1973a) 117 no. 381; (1978))
pop_text: The 1,500 seating capacity of the C4-C2 theatre (infra) gives some indication of population size in the late Classical period.
ha_text: The habitation area comprised about 9 ha.

chrono_text: According to Steph. Byz. 271.4, Henna was a foundation by Syracuse (no. 47) established in 664. While this may be evidence that a Greek identity was claimed for the city, the historicity of the report is doubtful. All evidence points to a Sikulan site strongly Heled: the archaeological evidence attests to some degree of Helation already from C6e.

The foundation date is uncertain, but the perspective of a powerful Selinous securing its eastern border towards Akragas as well as archaeological evidence (infra) suggest a C6m date. Hdt. 5.46.2 calls it an apoikia of Selinous; the oecist(s) and the date of foundation are unknown, but the date must be post-628 (date of Selinous' foundation).
ha_text: The city did occupy the area of the later Heltic town, but probably also, at least in part, the western plateau within the first phase circuit wall-in all c. 60-70 ha

chrono_text: the beginning of the expedition is connected with the destruction of Sybaris (no. 70) by Kroton (no. 56) in c. 510 (Hdt. 5.45; cf. Merante (1970a), (1970b) 131-33, who argues for an earlier chronology). According to Diod. 4.23.3, Dorieus did in fact succeed in taking back the land of the Herakleidai, and founded a polis named Herakleia which prospered for a period before being razed to the ground by the Carthaginians.

Greek C6e epigraphic material from Montagna di Marzo, the presumed site of Herbessos, includes votives to Herakles (cf. IGDS 166-68). Diod. 14.7.6 (r 404) suggests that it was fortified by C5l. The urban remains from this site are of C6m Sikel walls with C4 reinforcements in ashlar masonry.

The year of foundation, 648, is inferred from the notice that the city was inhabited for 240 years before its destruction in 409 (Diod. 13.62.4). Himera was pillaged and destroyed by the Carthaginians in 409; 3,000 surviving men were put to death, women and children were distributed to the victorious enemy and presumably enslaved (Diod. 13.59.4-62.5), and the site was abandoned (Diod. 11.49.4; Strabo 6.2.6), though there is some archaeological and numismatic evidence for re-occupation from c. 405 until the defeat of Dionysios I at Kronion in 383/2, when Himera became part of Carthaginian territory for good (Diod. 15.16.3).
pop_text: The C5e population has been calculated at 20,000, including a rural population of 10,000, by Asheri (1973), but this figure is probably on the low side, if one takes into account that the size of the urban centre of Himera is now calculated to be c. 80 ha (Allegro and Vassallo (1992) 147-48).
ha_text: the size of the urban centre of Himera is now calculated to be c. 80 ha (Allegro and Vassallo (1992) 147-48). The upper city occupied about 32 ha, the lower city about 50 ha (Allegro and Vassallo (1992) 145-47).

chrono_text: Rare C5m silver litrai have been attributed to Hippana; The urban remains are mainly from a C4m-C3m phase, which is interpreted as a Timoleontic refoundation of the originally indigenous settlement.
ha_text: Strongly built circuit walls in ashlar were raised on an acropolis and around the lower habitation plateau, 30 ha in area.

chrono_text: a C5 kerykeion inscribed 'Imacharaion damosion' (IG xiv 589; LSAG 269, 276.23, dating it to 475-450; IGDS no. 199, dating it to C5) was found in the region of modern Nissoria

The date of the foundation is unknown, but Ps.-Skymnos 286 mentions it alongside Naxos' other foundations (Leontinoi (no. 33), Zankle (no. 51) and Katane (no. 30)), and this may suggest that it was founded contemporaneously with them (i.e. C8l; M. Miller (1970) 35, 86). In Strabo's day it was deserted (6.2.6).

Kamarina was founded by Syracuse (no. 47), 135 years after the foundation of Syracuse itself, i.e. c. 598 (Thuc. 6.5.3); the date is confirmed by the archaeological evidence. In 484 Gelon destroyed Kamarina and transferred all Kamarinaians to Syracuse... Kamarina now probably ceased to exist until its refoundation by Gela in 461. In 461 (Diod. 11.76.5) Kamarina was refounded by Gela (Thuc. 6.5.3). A Timoleontic reinforcement of the population is reported by Diod. 16.82.7, and the archaeological evidence points to a revival of the city in C4s.
ha_text: During the C4s/Timoleontic phase the site comprised a fortified area of not less than 150 ha.
relocation_details: Kamarina was destroyed and refounded multiple times. In 484 Gelon destroyed Kamarina and transferred all Kamarinaians to Syracuse. In 461 Kamarina was refounded by Gela. Earlier, Hippokrates refounded the city c. 490. In 405 Dionysios I forced the inhabitants to relocate to Syracuse. The term used by Philistos and Timaios (= synoikizo) implies the participation in the new foundation of various unspecified groups.

chrono_text: Kasmenai was founded in 644/3 (Thuc. 6.5.3) by Syracuse (no. 47), probably as a colony, although there is no mention of an oecist.
pop_text: The sheer number of inhabitants, some 7-8,000 altogether, shows that the site must have been a proper town, and that some 2,000 soldiers max. must have been garrisoned in the town with their families.
ha_text: The settlement was founded on a high plateau (c. 70 ha), bounded by steep slopes and with no direct access to a hinterland.

chrono_text: Katane was founded by 'Thoukles and the Chalkidians from Naxos' (Thuc. 6.3.2; cf. Hellan. fr. 82), after these had founded Leontinoi in the fifth year after the foundation of Syracuse (i.e. 729: Thuc. 6.3.2).
pop_text: In 476, Hieron transferred the populations of Katane and Naxos to Leontinoi, renamed Katane as Aitna and settled it with 10,000 oiketores: 5,000 from the Peloponnese and 5,000 from Syracuse (Diod. 11.49.2)
ha_text: The central area of the Roman city may suggest the approximate extent of the Greek city. To the west this was delimited by the acropolis, to the north by the C6l-C5e cemetery (Orsi (1918)), to the north-east by the river Amenanos, and to the south-east by the sea, in all occupying an area of 75-100 ha (cf. Rizza (1996) 11-12).
relocation_details: In 476, Hieron transferred the populations of Katane and Naxos to Leontinoi, renamed Katane as Aitna and settled it with 10,000 oiketores. After Hieron had died in 466, the Katanaians returned in 461 and expelled the Aitnaians. In 403 Dionysios I took possession of Katane, the inhabitants were sold into slavery and the city granted to Campanians, who in their turn were transplanted to Aitna in 396. In 394, the surviving Katanaians were settled by Rhegion in Mylai.

After Timoleon ousted the tyrant Nikodemos in 339, the freed inhabitants were given Syracusan citizenship and possibly transferred to Syracuse. Kentoripa was presumably later assigned new citizens (a Timoleontic refoundation), reflected in the striking of a new coinage and craft production of Greek type.

chrono_text: C5l-C4e coins; Himilkon of Carthage concluded a treaty of friendship with Himera and 'those settled at the phrourion of Kephaloidion' (Diod. 14.56.2). it was captured by Dionysios I in 396

Leontinoi was one of the early Chalkidian colonies in Sicily, founded by Chalkidians from Naxos under the leadership of Theokles five years after the foundation of Syracuse (i.e. traditionally 729: Thuc. 6.3.3; cf. Hellan. fr. 82).
pop_text: in 396 Dionysios resettled Leontinoi with, allegedly, 10,000 mercenaries (Diod. 14.78.2).
ha_text: the total extent of the two plateaux with the central valley within the C6 circuit wall was about 40 ha.
relocation_details: Leontinoi experienced multiple population transfers: In 476, Hieron transplanted populations of Naxos and Katane to Leontinoi. After 424, civil war led to the demos being expelled and the dynatoi moving to Syracuse; the site became a phrourion of Syracuse. In 405/4 it was resettled by citizens of Gela and Kamarina. In 403/2 Dionysios conquered it and moved all inhabitants to Syracuse. In 396 Dionysios resettled it with mercenaries. In 339 Timoleon transplanted the population to Syracuse.
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, ekklesiastierion, theatre, law_courts

chrono_text: the foundation was the outcome of a joint Knidian-Rhodian expedition to western Sicily under the leadership of Pentathlos of Knidos, in the fiftieth Olympiad, i.e. 580-576. According to the Eusebian chronology, the foundation took place in 630/29, but Diodorus' date is supported by the chronology of the votive material from the urban sanctuary and by mortuary evidence
a C5m kerykeion inscribed; C5l coins; C5 evidence of Helation is found in the form of ashlar technique in the circuit wall

According to Thucydides the Megarians were driven out by Gelon (in 483) 245 years after the foundation, giving a foundation date of 728, thus later than the foundation of Syracuse. Archaeological evidence has revealed Greek presence at Megara from C8m. The city must have been refounded by Timoleon c. 338. the C3l fortification erected before the final destruction of the city by Marcellus in 213.
pop_text: On the assumption that about half of this area was urbanised, the C7 population has been estimated at c. 2,000 (De Angelis (1994) 97-99), probably a much too pessimistic assessment (Hansen (1997b) 74 n. 153).
ha_text: Megara was founded on a flat coastal plateau of c. 81 ha, previously uninhabited.
relocation_details: The expedition at first settled at Trotilos on the river Pantakyas, whence it moved on to Leontinoi and settled there with the Chalkidians for a while; it was, however, expelled and went on to settle at Thapsos. Here Lamis died; the remnants of the expedition left Thapsos and founded Megara on land put at their disposal by the Sikel king Hyblon. Later c.483 the demos was sold into slavery whereas the pachees were relocated to Syracuse and received citizenship there; Megara ceased to exist as a polis. The city must have been refounded by Timoleon c. 338.

chrono_text: By c. 460, Greek-style coinage was introduced with types that attest to Greek cults. When captured and refounded by Douketios in 459/8, it became an axiologos polis according to Diod. 11.78.5.
relocation_details: The Archaic town was situated on the Cittadella hill, whereas when captured and refounded by Douketios in 459/8, the new settlement on Serra Orlando was a city founded according to Greek customs with a formal urban layout.

chrono_text: Mylai was founded by Zankle (Ps.-Skymnos 287; cf. Strabo 6.2.6) in 716 (Euseb. Chron. sub Ol. 16.1). The earliest tombs are of C8l, thus confirming the Eusebian date (716) for the foundation.

The few C6-C5 and C4 urban remains, including a circuit wall built in an ashlar technique, are classified as Greek; the later phase with evidence of formal layout is probably Timoleontic. The main settlement was destroyed in C3f, but habitation continued on an adjoining terrace.
Nakone minted two series of bronze coins from C5l (Head, HN² 159)

It was founded by settlers from Chalkis (no. 365) on Euboia one year before the foundation of Syracuse, i.e. traditionally 735/4 (Thuc. 6.3.1). In 403 Naxos was betrayed by one of its own citizens, Prokles... He surrendered Naxos to Dionysios I, who subjected the city to an exandrapodismos from which it never recovered; the chora of the city was handed over to the Sikels (Diod. 14.15.2-3).
ha_text: Gates protected by towers were located in alignment with streets of the C7 city which covered an area of c. 35 ha.
relocation_details: In 476, Hieron of Syracuse transplanted the population of Naxos (and Katane) to Leontinoi, where they were presumably granted citizenship (Diod. 11.49.2), and it seems that he settled new colonists at Naxos. The expatriated Naxians presumably returned after Hieron's death. After the destruction in 403, Naxian survivors were settled at Mylai during 394, and in 358 the Naxians were finally settled at Tauromenion by Andromachos.

chrono_text: C4s coins; Diod. 23. 18. 5 (r 254) describes the Petrinoi as in possession of an urban centre, implicitly described as a polis.
C5s bronze coins; A silver litra of c. 400 is inscribed with the names of both Piakos and Adranon

According to Thuc. 6. 4. 2, Selinous was founded 100 years after the foundation of its metropolis Megara, i.e. c. 628/7. Diod. 13. 59. 4 supports the Eusebian date for the foundation of Selinous: 242 years before its destruction in 409/8, i.e. c. 651/50. The territorial disputes between Segesta and Selinous in 416 and 410 ultimately led to the destruction of Selinous in 409 by Carthage (Diod. 13. 55-57).
pop_text: a hint of the C5l size of population is found in the figures of 16,000 inhabitants killed and 5,000 captives taken by the Carthaginians in 409 (Diod. 13. 57. 6); this had already been preceded by the loss of 1,000 men in an ambush (Diod. 13. 44. 4 (r 410)). Even so, 2,600 escaped to Akragas (Diod. 13. 58. 3).
ha_text: The C6-C5 urban area comprised about 100 ha (Mertens (1989) fig. 4, (1999) fig. on p. 188).

chrono_text: The Stielanaioi are considered a Greek community by Mirone (1928), followed by Ziegler (1940), founded C5e in the hinterland of Katane (no. 30) and Leontinoi (no. 33) in the aftermath of the Deinomenid aggression against these cities.

Thuc. 6.3.2 gives the foundation date as 733/2 (supported by Eusebios, cf. Bérard (1957) 120 with refs.). However, a date c. C8m is indicated by Marmor Parium (FGrHist 239) A 31, which places Archias seven generations after Temenos and the return of the Herakleidai.
pop_text: Drögemüller (1969) 100 suggests an urban population of 40,000-45,000 for Syracuse, a guesstimate which may be a little on the high side in view of the size of the C5 habitation area, c. 150 ha (infra).
ha_text: from the time of the conflict with Athens, the size of the city was about 150 ha, and in the Heltic period as much as 200 ha.
relocation_details: Syracuse experienced many population relocations during the Classical period. Gelon transplanted and granted Syracusan citizenship to all Kamarinaians, half the Geloans, the pachees of Megara and Euboia, and over 10,000 mercenaries. Dionysios I enlarged the citizen body by enrolling freed slaves and inhabitants of Leontinoi, Kaulonia, and Hipponion. Timoleon relocated populations including people of Agyrion, Leontinoi, and settled thousands of new colonists. A koinon dogma reversed all population relocations of C5f.
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, bouleuterion, ekklesiastierion, theatre

chrono_text: So as a Greek polis Tauromenion was a C4f foundation; however, there is some archaeological evidence of C7 Greek presence at the site. If this Dionysian settlement of mercenaries was not in fact the foundation of the Greek polis (it may have struck coins, infra), then the polis must have come into being after this event and before the inscribing of the Delian inventories c. 374-367.
ha_text: The settlement comprises c. 65 ha, excluding the upper part of Monte Tauro above the city (perhaps a fortified acropolis).

chrono_text: Tyndaris was founded as a fortress by Dionysios I in 396 (Diod. 14. 78. 5)
pop_text: By admission of new settlers shortly after the foundation the population of Tyndaris grew to more than 5,000 (Diod. 14. 78. 6).
ha_text: The size of the city was about 14 ha.

chrono_text: Zankle was founded by Naxos (no. 41) as a sub-colony at the same time as Katane (no. 30) and Leontinoi (no. 33), i.e. c. 735-730. The date seems to be confirmed by the archaeological evidence
pop_text: Diod. 14.40.4 (r 399) mentions an army of 4,000 foot and 400 horse, in addition to thirty triremes.
ha_text: in all c. 50-60 ha (Vallet (1958) 113-16, pl. I; Scibona (1986))
relocation_details: The change of name from Zankle to Messana occurred c. 488/7 and was the result of tumultuous events: Anaxilas of Rhegion drove out the Samians, founded a city of mixed ethnicity, and changed the name to Messana after his own original homeland. In 396 Himilkon captured Messana, but in 395 the city was repopulated by Dionysios, who settled 1,000 Lokrians, 4,000 Medmaians and 600 Peloponnesian Messenians.

chrono_text: Diod. 12. 36. 4 dates the foundation to 433/2 and treats it as a purely Tarantine enterprise. Herakleia was a joint foundation of Taras (no. 71) and Thourioi (no. 74).
ha_text: Recently an overall, contemporary 433/2 occupation of the whole site has been argued, comprising an urban area of c. 140 ha
relocation_details: According to Antiochos, 'Herakleia' was not the original name: the city was thus named only after having changed its location, and a combination of Diodorus and Antiochos would suggest that the city was originally founded as Siris at the site of Siris, but later relocated and then named Herakleia.

chrono_text: Evidence from cemeteries and sanctuaries points to a C7l foundation date for Hipponion
ha_text: the extant remains of the c. 6 km-long circuit wall and the morphology of the site indicate a fortified area of about 80 ha; the urbanised area, presumably about the size of the mediaeval city, comprised about 40 ha
relocation_details: In 388 Hipponion was conquered by Dionysios I of Syracuse, who relocated the inhabitants to Syracuse, razed the city, and handed over its territory to the Lokrians. The Carthaginians later restored the city to the 'Hipponiates in exile' c. 379.

chrono_text: Hyele was founded by Phokaians (= Alalians (no. 1)) who had abandoned the Phokaian colony of Alalia on Corsica shortly after the battle of Alalia (c. 540-535) in which they had defeated Etruscan and Carthaginian forces but suffered severe losses themselves (Hdt. 1.166-67)
pop_text: A crew of eighty with families gives a figure of between 240 and 320 per ship, in all c. 4,800-6,400 persons (Gras (1985) 421-22); other calculations have reached a similar figure of c. 6,000 (Johannowsky (1982) 225).
ha_text: The walls run for c. 4 km and enclose an area of c. 64 ha, comprising the 'Nordstadt'; but during the early history of the site the habitation area was probably confined to the acropolis and the adjacent hill, and covered c. 9 ha.

chrono_text: The existence of a C6e circuit wall (Tréziny (1989) 129) points to a foundation in C7s, though Greek finds go back to C8l (Tréziny (1988) 205). In 389 Kaulonia was besieged and taken by Dionysios I of Syracuse (Diod. 14.103 ff)... the city was razed to the ground, and the territory handed over to Lokroi. A refoundation c. 357 by Dionysios II of Syracuse may be inferred from Diod. 16.10.2, 11.3.
ha_text: The Archaic walls therefore delimited an area of either 35-36 ha or 45-46 ha, the latter figure being the extent of the Heltic city.
relocation_details: In 389 Kaulonia was besieged and taken by Dionysios I of Syracuse. The citizens of Kaulonia were relocated to Syracuse, granted citizenship and exempted from taxes for five years; the city was razed to the ground, and the territory handed over to Lokroi. A refoundation c. 357 by Dionysios II of Syracuse may be inferred from Diod. 16.10.2, 11.3.

chrono_text: The colony was founded in 709/8 (Dion. Hal. Ant. Rom. 2.59.3; Jer. Chron. also gives Ol. 17.3, and the foundation is here treated as contemporary with that of Sybaris, as in other sources, e.g. Strabo 6.2.4, cf. 6.1.12; the Armenian Eusebios gives Ol. 17.4, i.e. 708/7).
pop_text: 100,000 men in 510 (Diod. 12.9.5). These, obviously, are unreliable figures, but it seems correct that, after the victory over Sybaris, Kroton had the largest population of the Greek cities in Italy (cf. Diod. 14.103.4 (r 389)); 10,000 citizens, the estimate of Beloch (1922) iii.1 306-7, may be correct.
ha_text: The fortified area comprised c. 620 ha (cf. Diod. 21.4 (r 295))

chrono_text: The archaeological evidence indicates Greek settlers at Kyme from c. 750-725, which is contemporary with the earliest Greek evidence from Pithekoussai. Kyme was the earliest of the Greek colonies in Italy and Sicily. in 421, Kyme fell to the Samnites, who subjected the city to an exandrapodismos and resettled it themselves.
pop_text: According to Dion. Hal. Ant. Rom. 7.3.4, Kyme in C6l, during the conflicts with the Etruscans, raised an army of 4,500 foot and 600 horse; the city also possessed a navy at this time.
ha_text: The walls enclosed an area of c. 80 ha, including the acropolis and part of the Monte Grillo which has revealed some traces of habitation in the Archaic period.

chrono_text: The date of foundation is unknown; the terminus ante quem for the existence of the Greek community is c. 510, when it commenced minting.

according to Eusebios at the time of Olympiad 25.1 or 26.4 (679/8 or 673/2). A foundation in the first decades of C7 is now confirmed by archaeological evidence. After a few years the colonists relocated to the historical site of Lokroi, with evidence of Greek settlers from c. 700, which fits Strabo's chronology (6.1.7) but is earlier than the Eusebian foundation date.
ha_text: The 7 km circuit wall encircled a vast area of c. 240 ha, of which the area south of the 'Dromos' comprises c. 80 ha.
relocation_details: The colonists first settled at Cape Zephyrion (modern Capo Bruzzano), but a few years later they moved to a permanent site at the 'hill of Epopis' with the co-operation of Syracusans and possibly of others.
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, prytaneion, theatre, stoa

chrono_text: Archaeological evidence points to a foundation in C7 (Paoletti (1981b) 147). Studies in the hinterland of Medma have revealed Greek influence from C7l.
pop_text: In 396, Dionysios I of Syracuse relocated 4,000 Medmaians to Sicilian Messana; The figure of 4,000 is the only indication of the order of size of the Medmaian population, and if it is historical, it probably refers to the entire population (Settis (1965) 125).
ha_text: The larger plateau had an extent of about 130-140 ha, but the urbanised area in its western part comprised only about 30-35 ha.
relocation_details: In 396, Dionysios I of Syracuse relocated 4,000 Medmaians to Sicilian Messana (Diod. 14.78.5); this probably meant a depopulation of Medma. When the populations of Kaulonia and Hipponion were relocated to Sicily in 389, Dionysios handed over their territories to Lokroi, and it is not impossible that the same happened in the case of Medma.

chrono_text: Euseb. Chron. Arm. under Ol. 1 dates the foundation to 733/2, but the archaeological evidence suggests a foundation c. 630
pop_text: On the basis of the seating capacity of the ekklesiasterion (infra), the total population of the polis may tentatively be estimated at max. 40,000, of whom some 12,500 could be accommodated in the town itself (Carter (1990) 406 with n. 2).
ha_text: A C6m circuit wall with extensive use of sun-dried brick was raised on the edge of the settlement plateau enclosing an area of 140-45 ha.

chrono_text: Metauros was a foundation either of Zankle or of Lokroi. The prevailing interpretation takes Metauros to be an originally Chalkidian, i.e. Zanklean, foundation later occupied by Lokroi, at about the time when that city founded Hipponion and Medma, i.e. C7s. Burials cease C6l, and very few tombs are known from C5. Such evidence may point to a termination of settlement C5l-C4e, perhaps due to Leukanian pressure.
ha_text: a conservative estimate is that the settlement comprised about 60 ha.

chrono_text: Neapolis was founded c. 470 on the initiative of Kyme (no. 57; Ps.-Skymnos 242-43; cf. Strabo 5.4.7); the date is based on the chronology of Syracusan involvement in Kymaian history and upon evidence from the Castel Capuano cemetery (Frederiksen (1984) 94, 101-7; Valenza Mele (1993) 197-98).
pop_text: E. Greco (1985a) 199-216 estimates two rows of ten houses per insula, with seven or eight inhabitants per house, and hence an urban population of 1,000-8,000 inhabitants.
ha_text: The plateau was fortified by a circuit wall c. 3.8 km long and enclosing an area of c. 75 ha

chrono_text: According to Eusebios (Helm 181; Schöne 78), the foundation of Pandosia was contemporary with that of Metapontion: Ol. 1.4 = 773; it is not, however, clear whether the reference is to this Pandosia or the one near Herakleia. In any case, the Eusebian date for Metapontion is unreliable.

the archaeological data from the 'Gosetti-Akropolis dump' and from the Mazzola excavation date the foundation to c. 750 (Neeft (1994) 150 n. 9 ('c. 740'); Coldstream (1995); d'Agostino (1999a) 56-58).
pop_text: Within the first generations after the foundation the population may have risen to 5,000-10,000 (Ridgway (1992) 101-3). Morris (1996) 57 suggests a minimum population of 4,000-5,000.
ha_text: The whole area of Pithekoussai, including the Valle di S. Montano with cemeteries, and the lowland plain, with the probable harbour, between Monte di Vico and the ridge of Mezzavia, comprises about 75 ha (Buchner (1975) 66). The plateau, relatively flat, formed a habitation area of about 6 ha.

chrono_text: The literary tradition offers only a terminus ante quem for the foundation, viz. c. 530, provided by Herodotos, who at 1.167.4 refers to the city as in existence when Hyele (no. 54) was founded (c. 540-535). Archaeological evidence suggests a foundation date of c. 600.
pop_text: The C5m-C4 circular ekklesiasterion had a seating capacity of 1,000-1,700.
ha_text: The extent of the C6-C5 habitation area, estimated at c. 125 ha, is indicated by several large cemeteries and sanctuaries situated along the edge of the urban area.

chrono_text: Diod. 11.59.4, where it is reported that it was founded as a polis in 471/0 by Mikythos, the tyrant of Rhegion (no. 68) and Zankle/Messana (no. 51); C6s coins inscribed PYXOES may belong to this city; If the coins were so minted, it follows that Pyxous existed prior to Mikythos' foundation of the site
ha_text: The walls enclosed an area of about 11 ha, of which perhaps only about 6 ha was used for habitation.

chrono_text: It was one of the early Chalkidian foundations, probably founded shortly after Naxos and Zankle (730-720) as corroborated by C8l and C7 ceramic finds. The participation of Messenian refugees from the First Messenian War dates the foundation to C8s.
pop_text: According to Hdt. 7.170.3, Rhegion lost 3,000 men in battle against the Iapygians in 473. An army of 6,000 foot, 600 horse and fifty triremes was deployed in 399.
ha_text: The area within the C4 circuit wall comprised about 70 ha, obviously less during the Archaic period if part of the Aspropomonte hills were outside the fortified area.

chrono_text: the archaeological evidence, which points to a foundation date of C8l-C7e. The foundation date of c. 660 implied by the synchronisation with Gyges' capture of Kolophon. So, by the 430s Siris had been replaced by Herakleia (no. 52). Diod. 12. 36. 4 dates the foundation of this colony to 433/2, and he reports that it was founded at the site of Siris, whose inhabitants were deported.
Sybaris was founded in the last quarter of C8 (721/20: Ps.Skymnos 360; or 709/8: Euseb. Chron. 91b Helm), a date confirmed by archaeological evidence
pop_text: The large figures reported for the number of citizens (100,000 astoi: Ps.-Skymnos 341; 300,000 politai: Diod. 10.23, 12.9.2) or armed forces (300,000 men: Diod. 10.23 and Strabo 6.1.13; 50,000 horse: Ath. 519C = Timaeus (FGrHist 566) fr. 50), although obviously legendary, may all the same indicate a very populous city; the size of the urban area (c. 500 ha, infra) confirms that Sybaris was indeed a large city.
ha_text: recent investigations indicate that the extent of the city was about 500 ha.
relocation_details: Sybaris went through multiple phases: Sybaris I was destroyed by Kroton in 510. Sybaris II continued as a dependent polis of Kroton. Sybaris III was refounded c.453 by Thessalos but destroyed again by the Krotoniates. Sybaris IV was the initial Sybarite-Athenian settlement that became Thourioi. The expelled Sybarites then settled by the river Traeis (Sybaris on the Traeis), which was eventually destroyed by the Brettians.

chrono_text: founded in C8l (706 according to Euseb. Chron. 91, Helm; cf. Van Compernolle (1992) 774). the archaeological evidence confirms the traditional C8l date for the foundation (Lo Porto (1970) 357-58; De Juliis (1983) 429; Boschung (1994) 177).
pop_text: Allegedly, Taras could muster 30,000 infantry, 3,000 cavalry and 1,000 hipparchoi (Strabo 6.3.4) in the age of Archytas. Diodorus has 20,000 infantry and 2,000 cavalry (20.104.2 (r 303)) as the citizen troops mobilised by Kleonymos in 303. And Taras was known as the most populous of the cities of Magna Graecia (Ps.-Skymnos 330). If the army figures are of the right order of magnitude, Taras, at its peak in C4, must have had a citizen population of over 100,000 plus an unknown number of foreigners and slaves (on the basis of these army figures, Wuilleumier (1939) 173-74 calculates a C4m citizen population of 240,000 and a C4l one of 155,000; Beloch (1886) 302 suggests 60,000 for C3l).
ha_text: The large extent of the C5-C4 city, c. 530 ha, may support a high population figure, though the intramural area included the cemetery (Polyb. 8.28.7). From C5s the urban area measured c. 530 ha (E. Greco (1981b) 150-51); a c. 10 km-long circuit wall built in 450-430 incorporated the new urban extension and the Archaic cemeteries.
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, bouleuterion, prytaneion, ekklesiastierion, theatre

chrono_text: the cultural assemblage is indeed C6s-C5f Greek; Initially, i.e. C6l, it was an incuse coinage with the tripod of Kroton; prior to 510 within the dominion of Sybaris and after 510 in the dominion of Kroton, and from c. 480, some time after the Medmean, Hipponian and Lokrian defeat of Kroton, in the dominion of Lokroi

Terina was founded by Kroton; the date of foundation is unknown, and the terminus ante quem is c. 460, the date at which the city began issuing coins. Terina may have been founded after the Krotoniate defeat of Sybaris c. 510. Terina was besieged and taken by the Brettians in 356, and the city became a Brettian community.
The foundation proper of Thourioi followed in 444/3 (in the archonship of Praxiteles: Ps.-Plut. X orat. 835 D; cf. Dion. Hal. Lys. 1.2).
pop_text: Nothing is known of the size of the population, the only available figures being army numbers.

chrono_text: Adria was possibly a C6l foundation of Aigina (no. 358). In C4f Adria was, apparently, refounded by Dionysios I.
According to Strabo 5.4.2, Ankon was colonised c. 387 by Syracusan refugees who had fled from Dionysios I

according to Plut. Mor. 552 E the contingent was led by Periander, which suggests a date of c. 600 for the foundation. The tradition mentioning Periander indicates that the foundation took place during his reign, although his personal involvement remains uncertain; foundation chronology c. 600. The lack of Protocorinthian pottery confirms a foundation date of c. 600.
ha_text: A C6 ? ashlar circuit wall fortified the acropolis and upper settlement area (c. 20 ha). The lower city (c. 110 ha) was protected by an extensive C4-C3 ashlar and brick circuit wall.

chrono_text: Brentesion is called a polis in late sources only, in Polyb. 10.1.9 referring to the Roman colony of C3m and retrospectively in Strabo 6.3.6 referring to the mythical period. However, its status as a Greek or at least partly Hellenized polis of the Classical period is indicated by a C5m kerykeion.

Epidamnos was founded in either 627 or 625 (Hieron. Chron. 97 b, Helm) by Korkyraians (no. 123) (Thuc. 1. 24. 1; Ps.-Skymnos 435-36) assisted by some Corinthians (no. 227) and other Dorians (Thuc. 1. 24. 2). The few Greek remains lie beneath the Roman, mediaeval and modern city, but they confirm a foundation date of c. 625
Its origin is unknown, though most scholars take it to be one of the Adriatic foundations of Dionysios I (see coin evidence below). Herakleia struck bronze coins in C4 on the Syracusan standard.

Issa is normally believed to have been founded by Dionysios I in connection with his establishment of colonies in the Adriatic (Diod. 15. 13. 1-5 (r 385)). The present archaeological record indicates that there was no Greek settlement before c. 330, but the archaeological record is not necessarily incompatible with a foundation date in the reign of Dionysios I.
ha_text: The C4 wall enclosed an area of c. 9.8 ha.

chrono_text: Lissos was founded shortly before 385 by Dionysios I (Diod. 15.13.4)
ha_text: a C4 phase comprised only an upper acropolis of c. 2.3 ha. Walls in ashlar blocks, strengthened with square towers, were raised down the slope of the hill to the river Drin in C3-C2; with a perimeter of 2,150 m they enclosed a lower settlement area of c. 22 ha

chrono_text: Melaina Korkyra was a Knidian colony. Some historians assume that there were two colonies on the island, the first one founded by Knidians in perhaps C6 (Beaumont (1936) 174) ... The foundation by Issaians is attested in the inscription ... now dated to C3f (Woodhead (1970) 508-9 following Rendic-Miocevic (1983) 192) or C4-C3 (Fraser (1993) 170 ff and LGPN 3)
relocation_details: The original Knidian settlement had died out before the new Issaian foundation, and the Issaians were resettling a place already occupied in the past by the Greeks. The Issaian foundation may have been located at Lumbarda, in eastern Korcula.

chrono_text: Pharos was a colony (apoikia) founded by Paros (no. 509) in 385 with the assistance of Dionysios I of Syracuse according to Diod. 15.13.4, 14.1-2
ha_text: the size of the Greek city is estimated to comprise between 1.5 ha and 6 ha (Kirigin (1990) 299; Cambi (2002) 56-57)

chrono_text: The archaeological record indicates that the period referred to is C6l-C4. By C4m the city fell victim to Celtic invasions.
ha_text: The habitation area extended over c. 6 ha and was linked to the sea by a 15 m wide canal, probably built by Etruscan engineers.

chrono_text: it was fortified in C5
ha_text: the walls were about 2,100 m long and enclosed an area of roughly 20 ha.

The earliest evidence of Batiai as a polis is Theopomp. fr. 206, where Batiai is mentioned together with Elateia (no. 94), Pandosia (no. 104) and Boucheta (no. 90) as poleis ton Kassopaion.
ha_text: It was 1,400 m in circumference and enclosed an area of 10.1 ha

chrono_text: Berenike was a polis founded by Pyrrhos II in C3s (Steph. Byz. 164.3: App. Mithr. 4). The place, however, was fortified already in C4s. Therefore the precursor of Berenike can perhaps be recognised as a dependent polis from C4s, viz. a dependency of Kassopa.

The site was settled from prehistoric times. Corvisier (1991) 201 suggests that, already in the Archaic period, 0.75 ha was fortified. By C5l an isodomic wall with eight towers had been built and enclosed an area of 1.5 ha.
pop_text: When in C4f the settlement extended westward, the wall with interval towers was increased and now enclosed up to 3.5 ha, with a population of about 1,000.
ha_text: When in C4f the settlement extended westward, the wall with interval towers was increased and now enclosed up to 3.5 ha, with a population of about 1,000.

chrono_text: Bouthroton is attested as a polis in the urban sense at Hecat.fr. 106 ; it is unlikely that there was a polis in Epeiros as early as 500 , unless it was a Greek colony (Hansen ( 1997 ) 20 ). There is no tradition of a Greek colony,but excavations have yielded Protocorinthian,Corinthian and Attic pottery of C7 to C5.
ha_text: The walls surrounded 3 . 7 ha (Corvisier ( 1991 ) 287 ).

chrono_text: The Bylliones acted for the first time as a political unit c. C4m, when they posed an oracle inquiry to Dodone. The town of Byllis was situated at the right side of the Aoos, at modern Gradisht. It was fortified in C4m.
ha_text: The wall enclosed an area of 28 ha (Corvisier (1991) 288).
The acropolis was surrounded by a C4 polygonal wall which enclosed an area of 3.4 ha

chrono_text: This site was settled from prehistoric times.
pop_text: Dakaris (ibid.) 172 suggests that in C5l the settlement was fortified with a polygonal wall of 1,690 m in circumference, which enclosed an area of 12.7 ha corresponding to a population of about 3,800.

chrono_text: the archaeological, numismatic and epigraphical record reveals that in C4s Elea must have been a polis. For a short period between c. 360 and 335 Elea struck bronze coins
ha_text: Dakaris (1972) 97 identifies Elea with Charauge (near modernVeliani) on the slopes of the Korila and describes it as a fortified settlement of 10 ha

chrono_text: This site was inhabited from the prehistoric period and was already in LH III a fully developed settlement with a fortified acropolis enclosing an area of 4.24 ha; the settlement was the same size in C4.
ha_text: a fortified acropolis enclosing an area of 4.24 ha (Dakaris (1972) 80 fig. 40); the settlement was the same size in C4

chrono_text: reporting the siege, conquest and destruction of the polis in 312
ha_text: The area enclosed by the circuit measures c. 34.4 ha (Corvisier (1991) 214; Vokotopoulou (1970)).

chrono_text: A C4m manumission inscription naming the eponymous prostates of the Thesprotians testifies to the importance of this town already in the Classical period and shows that Gitana belonged to the Thesprotian tribal community (SEG 26 717).
ha_text: In C4 an area of 28 ha was enclosed with a circuit wall running for 3,000 m (Dakaris (1972) 115)

chrono_text: Excavations show that Horraon was fortified before C4m. some houses of C4m-s have been excavated.
ha_text: The enclosed area measures c. 7.2 ha (Corvisier (1991) 284).

chrono_text: From C4m Kassopa was the political centre of the region and is recorded in the C4m list of theorodokoi from Epidauros. The C4m defence circuit enclosed an area of 30 ha. A temple of Aphrodite was built about C4m.
ha_text: The C4m defence circuit enclosed an area of 30 ha (Hoepfner and Schwandner (1994) 123).

chrono_text: The only reason to include Phanote as a possible polis in the Classical period is the combination of the Heltic site-classification with the remains of a fairly large C4l settlement

The site was settled from the prehistoric period. In C4, the settlement on the hill was fortified and the walls enclosed an area of c. 34.4 ha.

chrono_text: Ambrakia was founded by Corinthians (no. 227) (Thuc. 2.80.3; Dem. 3.34) at the time of Kypselos (Strabo 7.6.6, 10.2.8; Ant. Lib. Met. 4.4), i.e. between 657/6 and 627/6 (for the date of Kypselos, see Gehrke (1990) 34).
pop_text: at Plataiai with 500 hoplites (Hdt. 9.28.5, 31.4); In 425, Ambrakia went to war against Akarnania and Amphilochian Argos (no. 115) with at least 3,000 hoplites (Thuc. 3.105.1)
ha_text: The walls mentioned by Livy (38.4.4) belong primarily to the time of Pyrrhos; they enclose an area of c. 130 ha, but there are remains of the older walls of late Archaic or Classical times

chrono_text: Anaktorion was founded by Corinthians at the time of Kypselos (657/6-627/6; for the date, see Gehrke (1990) 34; Strabo 10.2.8; Nic. Dam.(FGrHist 90) fr. 57.7).
pop_text: In the battle of Plataiai, Anaktorion was present, together with Leukas (no. 126), with 800 hoplites (Hdt. 9.28.5).

relocation_details: In about 440 the Amphilochians were expelled by Ambrakian settlers, but some two years later, with Athenian aid, they were resettled together with Akarnanians in Argos, while the Ambrakian settlers were exposed to andrapodismos (Thuc. 2.68.5-7).

chrono_text: If the identification with the ruins of Skourtou is correct, Derion had an urban centre and may have been a polis already in C5-C4
As a member of the Akarnanian Confederacy which had an urban centre and presumably struck coins, Echinos was probably a polis, at least from C4

Since remains of a Classical and Heltic urban settlement with a cemetery exist west of the above-mentioned Late Heltic/Roman site of Saradiniko (information from E.-L. Schwandner), Euripos was probably a polis, at least from C4.
the inscription on the golden phiale of C7-C6 found in Olympia (SEG 1 94; cf. supra) is connected with Pliny's Heraclia by Pritchett (1992) 100-1

Hyporeiai is attested only twice: as a toponym in an Epidaurian theorodokoi list, and on an Athenian grave stele from the Piraeus. Hyporeiai must have been a member of the Akarnanian Confederacy in C4.
Polis status in C6 may be inferred from the cult of the goddess Athena Polias (IG ix².1 1614 (C6))
ha_text: The city walls enclose an area of c. 12 ha.

chrono_text: Strabo 6.2.4 says that it was founded in the same year as Syracuse (no. 47), i.e. traditionally in 733; Euseb. Chron. (translated by Jerome), however, gives the year 706, which better fits the archaeological evidence
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, bouleuterion, prytaneion, law_courts

chrono_text: From the account of Thucydides (especially 2.102.1) we have to conclude that Koronta belonged to the Akarnanian Confederacy already in C5. It is explicitly attested as a member in C3 (IG ix².1 583, 582).

Kranioi is referred to as a polis in the urban sense by the term tetrapalis at Thuc. 2.30.2; silver coins of C5-C4. Polygonal and ashlar walls exist on three sides of the town, for Mycenaean remains and those of C5-C4.
Leukas was founded during the reign of the tyrant Kypselos (Strabo 10.2.8, for the date (657/6-627/6), see Gehrke (1990) 34; Oberhummer (1887) 76 proposed c. 635) by Corinthians
pop_text: at Plataiai together with Anaktorion (no. 114) with 800 hoplites (Hdt. 9.28.5)

chrono_text: in C5s, Limnaia was a kome belonging to Argos (no. 115), but by 356/5 it had become a political unit and acquired the status of polis.

*Matropolis is not attested as a polis before C3, when the term is found in the urban sense in Polyb. 4.64.4 (r 219) and in the political sense in an inscription concerning a boundary dispute between Oiniadai (no. 130) and *Matropolis found in Aitolian Thermos (IG ix².1 3 B.8 (C3)). But the polis status of *Matropolis in the Classical period is strongly indicated by the toponym itself, the coinage of C4 (see infra), the membership of both the Aitolian and the Akarnanian Confederacies (see infra), and the existence of an urban centre at the site of Palaiomanina

Medion is not attested as a polis before Polyb. 2.2.7, 11, 2.3.1 and 6 (r 231), where the term is found in the urban sense. But in theorodokoi lists of C4 it is recorded under the heading Akarnania, it is attested as a member of the Akarnanian Confederacy in C3, it struck coins and had a walled urban centre... So it was probably a polis already in the Classical period.

Messenians from Naupaktos (no. 165) attacked Oiniadai in 456/5 and forced its population to leave their town, but in the next year the Oiniadai returned. In 325/4, the Oiniadai were driven out by the Aitolians. On Kassander's advice the Oiniadai left their settlement in 314 and moved to Sauria.
ha_text: It enclosed the acropolis and the lower city, an area of altogether 59 ha, and may date from C5-C4
relocation_details: On Kassander's advice the Oiniadai left their settlement in 314 and moved to Sauria (Diod. 19.67.4). Strabo 10.2.2 mentions an old Oiniadai at a greater distance from the sea.

chrono_text: Palairos was probably a polis already in C5: it was a member of the Akarnanian Confederacy, it had an urban centre, and it seems to have struck coins
It was probably a polis already in C5: it was a member of the Akarnanian Confederacy and it had an urban centre
Pronnoi is commonly identified with the site on the Pachni hill, where there are some remains of a Classical and Heltic settlement (C5-C3)
a small fortress of polygonal masonry at Pyrgos (C5-C3); Same struck various silver and bronze coins from C5 onward.
Sollion was conquered by the Athenians in 431/0 and handed over to the polis of Palairos... The presumption is that Sollion thereafter was the harbour of Palairos and had ceased to be a polis.

The oldest attested public enactment is the proxeny decree of C5 (IG ix².1 390). According to Thuc. 2.81.2, Stratos was a walled city in 429, which suggests that the existing enceinte dates from at least C5.
pop_text: The C4 theatre of Stratos (now excavated) accommodated c. 8,000 spectators.
ha_text: The circuit of walls built of pseudo-isodomic masonry and surrounding both the fortified acropolis and the town was about 7.5 km long and enclosed an area of c. 80 ha.
relocation_details: In 314, on the advice of Cassander, many Akarnanians moved to Stratos (Diod. 19.67.4).
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, bouleuterion, theatre, stoa

chrono_text: A sanctuary of Zeus Meilichios is attested in a C5 horos inscription. Thyrreion was a member of the Akarnanian Confederacy, had a walled urban centre and struck coins in C4; so it was probably a polis in the Classical period.

If one accepts the identification of the ruins of Komboti with Torybeia and the interpretation of the coins given below, there can be little doubt that Torybeia was a polis already in the Classical period.
It seems reasonable to infer that Agrinion must have been a polis during the last decades of the Classical period.
relocation_details: In 314, King Kassander of Macedon convened an ekklesia of the Akarnanians and persuaded them to leave the small and unfortified settlements and move into a few poleis of which Agrinion is explicitly mentioned. As a result the Derieis and other Akarnanians were synoecised into Agrinion.

chrono_text: Thucydides calls Aigition a polis in the urban sense, describing how the inhabitants found refuge in the hills above the city... in 426
The only thing we know about Akripos is that in, probably, 355 a theorodokos was appointed to host theoroi from Epidauros.

On the coast, upon the hill Hag. Triadha at modern Kato Vasiliki, are the remains of a defence circuit and some houses belonging to a settlement of the Classical and Heltic periods. Above the city on the southern slope of M. Varassova are substantial remains of a C5?-C4 fortress.
Kallipolis was founded in C4m at the latest, and was from the outset laid out as a planned city.
ha_text: Excavations have revealed substantial remains of a defence circuit (enclosing an area of c. 25 ha)

chrono_text: the temples of Artemis Laphria (C7, rebuilt several times); the sanctuary of Apollo Laphrios is attested through a C6 boundary stone; The earliest epigraphical reference to a polis Kalydoniôn is on a C2 statue base. In the Iliad Kalydon is described as a polis in the Heroic Age.
ha_text: The walls enclosed an area of c. 25 ha.

chrono_text: Strabo criticises Hellanikos (fr. 118) for classifying Makynea as a polis founded in the Heroic Age. That Makynea was a polis in the Classical period is indicated by a C4 proxeny decree from Delphi.

In C6/C5 Molykreion was within the Corinthian sphere of influence, but by C5m it had passed into the Athenian sphere and from, probably, C5l it was under Aitolian influence. Thucydides (3.102.2) describes Molykreion as a Corinthian colony that had become an Athenian satellite.

The only evidence that it was already a polis in the Classical period is the name Tityros Pholaieus, recorded in two Attic sepulchral inscriptions (IG ii² 10036 (C5l-C4e), 10482) where the individual and external use of the ethnic indicates that it is a city-ethnic rather than a sub-ethnic
in, probably, 355 a theorodokos was appointed to host theoroi from Epidauros

In the Catalogue of Ships Pleuron is recorded as an Aitolian city (Il. 2.639). Pleuron was originally an independent polis. In C5/C4 it became-like Kalydon-a member of the early Achaian Federation. In C4m/s it became integrated into the Aitolian Federation. In C3s Pleuron was destroyed, but the city was soon rebuilt at a nearby but more elevated place.
relocation_details: In C3s Pleuron was destroyed, but the city was soon rebuilt at a nearby but more elevated place (Strabo 10.2.4). Old Pleuron was situated on the coast between the rivers Acheloos and Euenos near Kalydon. New Pleuron has been identified with the ancient settlement at modern Kato Retsina, on the southern side of Mt. Arakynthos.

chrono_text: theorodokoi were appointed to host theoroi from Epidauros (IG iv² 95.38), and a citizen of Proschion is honoured in a Delphic proxeny decree of the early Heltic period. Combined, the two sources indicate that Proschion was a polis, at least in the late Classical period.
relocation_details: According to Strabo (10.2.6) Pylene-mentioned in the Homeric Catalogue of Ships (Il. 2.639)-was later moved to a more elevated place and renamed Proschion.

chrono_text: the only thing we know about Therminea is that in, probably, 355 a theorodokos was appointed to host theoroi from Epidauros

that Trichoneion was a polis and a member of the Aitolian Federation already in C4f is implied by an Athenian decree of 367 in which the Trichoneians are charged with having broken a sacred truce (Tod 137).
Thuc. 3.101.2, where... in 426, the Alpaioi had to provide hostages to the Peloponnesian League alongside a number of other Lokrian communities

In 480 Amphissa served as a place of refuge for the Phokians and the Delphians (Hdt. 8.32.2, 36.2). Amphissa is then not mentioned again in the sources until the Peloponnesian War. However, in spite of Strabo 9.4.8 (cf. 9.3.4), it seems that the city was not destroyed in 338 (Lerat (1952) ii. 53-54).
ha_text: There are still remains of the ancient acropolis wall, built partly in Lesbian masonry and enclosing an area of 1 ha.

chrono_text: IG ix².1 718.47 (C5f); IG ix².1 717.7 (C5m); attesting to the participation of Chaleion in the C5f colonisation of Naupaktos
ha_text: The defence circuit at this site encloses an area of 8 ha and dates from C4l or C3e

chrono_text: Among the Lokrian peoples who were in contact with the Spartan Eurylochos in 426, Thucydides mentions the Hyaioi. In the Heltic period the Hyaioi and the Polieis were distinct political communities, and each was a polis: c. 200 the polis of the Hyaians set up a statue in Thermos.

Hypnia is explicitly called a polis in the C2e sympoliteia with neighbouring Myania (F.Delphes iii.4 352.ii.4-5). The Heltic evidence should be combined, retrospectively, with Thucydides' information at 3.101.2 that, in 426, the Hypnians had to provide hostages to the Peloponnesian League alongside a number of other Lokrian communities.

The attestation of a Messapian treasurer of 337/6 should be combined, retrospectively, with Thucydides' information at 3.101.2 that, in 426, the Messapians had to provide hostages to the Peloponnesian League alongside a number of other Lokrian communities

Myania is attested as a polis in the sympoliteia with Hypnia (no. 161) of c. 190. The main reason for classifying Myania as a polis in the Archaic and Classical periods is Thucydides' information at 3.101.2 that, in 426, the Myonians had to provide hostages to the Peloponnesian League alongside a number of other Lokrian communities.
ha_text: remains of an ancient cemetery and of a defence circuit enclosing an area of c. 8 ha

chrono_text: According to Ps.-Skymnos 479, Naupaktos was a Dorian polis founded in the Heroic Age by Temenos. In C5f contingents of colonists were sent to Naupaktos from East Lokris and Chaleion.
pop_text: provided strong contingents of hoplites, testifying to the large size of the population (500 hoplites in 427 (Thuc. 3.75.1), 600 in 410 (Diod. 13.48.6)).

chrono_text: The earliest attestation of Oianthea as a polis in the political sense is in a C2 manumission inscription (IG ix².1 709 a.2); that it was a polis community in the Classical period as well is apparent from the treaty with Chaleion of C5m. The individual and external use is found on a C7-C6 cenotaph of a Korkyrean proxenos (ML 4.2).

Tolophon is attested as a polis in the political sense in an inscription of C2m (IG ix².1 715) found in situ at Vidavi/Marmara and thus identifying the site. The reason for including Tolophon in this inventory of Archaic and Classical poleis is Thucydides' information at 3.101.2
in 426, the Triteians had to provide hostages to the Peloponnesian League alongside a number of other Lokrian communities
ha_text: Tritea was situated at modern Pendeoria, where there is an undated defence circuit enclosing an area of c. 1 ha

chrono_text: The remains found in the neighbouring sanctuary and in the cemetery date back to C7
ha_text: Both the acropolis and the lower town were fortified, and the walls enclosed an area of c. 16 ha.

chrono_text: Herodotos' account at 8.35.1 of Xerxes' march in 480 from Phanoteus towards Delphi when *Aiolidai was burnt to the ground

The earliest attestation of Ambryssos as a polis is of C2 (IG ix.1 10.1-2), but it is retrospectively attested as a polis in the urban sense at Paus. 10.3.2, where it is listed among the Phokian cities dioikised in 346. If one accepts the historicity of this war (but cf. Ellinger (1993) 17-22, contra Pritchett (1996)) we have here the oldest attestation of Ambryssos as a polis.

Amphikaia was burnt to the ground in connection with Xerxes' invasion of Phokis in 480. Pausanias treats Amphikaia as a polis once again in connection with the dioikismos of the Phokian cities in 346 after the Third Sacred War.
ha_text: The almost quadrangular fortification wall (c. 250 m (east-west) × c. 200 m (north-south))

chrono_text: A bronze statuette found in a sanctuary of Athena that was probably situated in the territory of Antikyra has a dedicatory inscription which, according to the editor, is 'Archaic': (C6-C5?). Excavations have revealed a sanctuary of Athena of the Archaic and Classical periods (BCH 89 (1955) 257 (C6f)).

With Herakleia as a possible analogy (Thuc. 3. 92), it is tempting to connect the foundation with the strained relations between Doris and its neighbours in C5 (Thuc. 1. 107). But that remains a hypothesis. The inference seems to be that Boulis was a polis in the years 356-354.

Charadra is called a polis in the urban sense at Hdt. 8.33 and, retrospectively, at Paus. 10.3.2, in both passages in connection with Xerxes' invasion of Phokis in 480, when Charadra was burnt to the ground. Pausanias treats Charadra as a polis once again in connection with the dioikismos of the Phokian cities in 346 after the Third Sacred War.
ha_text: The northern part is too poorly preserved to estimate the area enclosed by the city wall.

chrono_text: Daulis is called polis in the urban sense by Hdt. 8.35.1 in connection with Xerxes' invasion of Greece in 480, when Daulis was burnt to the ground. Daulis is recorded by Paus. 10.3.2 among the cities exposed to dioikismos in 346. A terminus ante quem is, in any case, 198, when Daulis was conquered by the Romans (Livy 32.18.7).
ha_text: The undated remains of the ancient defence circuit enclose an area of c. 200 × 250 m

chrono_text: evidence of a settlement within the sanctuary can be traced back to C10, and remains of a house have been dated to C8m
pop_text: When the people voted with pebbles,the decree passed sometimes records the number of votes cast, and three such decrees are preserved: the first records a total of 454 votes (F.Delphes iii.1 294.i.2-3 (C4f)), the second over 400 votes (CID i 13.35-37 (C4f)), and the third 353 votes
ha_text: The sanctuary (2.3 ha) is densely built and centres on the so-called Sacred Way which winds its way down the slopes of the temenos.
monuments: agora, temple, bouleuterion, prytaneion, theatre, gymnasium, law_courts

chrono_text: Drymos is called a polis in the urban sense at Hdt. 8.33 and, retrospectively, at Paus. 10.3.1 and 2, in both passages in connection with Xerxes' invasion of Phokis in 480, when Drymos was burnt to the ground. Paus. 10.3.2 treats Drymos as a polis in the urban sense in connection with the dioikismos of the Phokian cities in 346 after the Third Sacred War.
ha_text: The upper part enclosed an area of c. 20 ha (Frazer (1898) 423-24; Dassios (1992) no. 2).

chrono_text: Echedameia is listed by Paus. 10.3.2 among the poleis (in the urban sense) exposed by Philip to dioikismos in 346.

According to a tradition reported by Paus. 8.4.4, 10.34.2-3 and attested already in C2, Elateia was founded by the Arkadian Elatos, the son of Arkas. In 346 it was one of the cities dioikised by Philip.
The cemetery excavated at KatoSouvala contains remains of C8 onwards.

The recent excavations of the sanctuary of Artemis Elaphebolia and Apollo have revealed continuous habitation and utilisation from the Mycenaean age (LH iii C) to the Byzantine period. Hyampolis is called a polis in the urban sense by Hdt. 8. 33 in connection with Xerxes' invasion of Phokis in 480, when Hyampolis was burnt to the ground. Hyampolis is listed as one of the poleis exposed to dioikismos in 346.
ha_text: A circuit wall encloses an area of some 280 m (north-south) by 200 m (east-west).

chrono_text: The state of the evidence speaks against accepting Kirrha as a polis destroyed in C6e. On the contrary, the sources seem to substantiate the opposite view: that, in the Archaic period down to C6e, there was no polis of the Kirrhaioi whose territory had been consecrated to Apollo. The polis status of Krisa/Kirrha in C6e is an aetiological invention which became prominent in the context of the Third and Fourth Sacred Wars in order to explain the obscure origin of the hiera ge.

Ledon was one of the poleis exposed to dioikismos in 346 (10.3.2). Pausanias reports that Ledon had once been a polis, but that in his time the few surviving Ledontians had abandoned their polis and settled near the Kephisos.
ha_text: At Modi are the remains of a small fortification (100 × 100 m) in isodomic masonry

chrono_text: After the dioikismos of 346 (Paus. 10. 3. 2) Lilaia was refounded (Paus. 10. 33. 3). The coins, the dioikismos in 346, and the recording of a Lilaian witnessing the payment of the Phokians' fine in 337/6 (IG ix. 1 111) are sufficient proof that Lilaia was a member of the Phokian koinon in C5 and C4.
relocation_details: After the dioikismos of 346 (Paus. 10. 3. 2) Lilaia was refounded (Paus. 10. 33. 3). Additionally, the Archaic site may have been on a hill c. 1 km north-west of the C4 fortifications, with remains of a wall in polygonal masonry perhaps of C6, suggesting the polis may have moved from the earlier site to the Classical location.

chrono_text: Medeon disappears as a polis and becomes a phratria of Stiris. The cemetery was abandoned in C3m, according to Robert and Robert (BE (1970) 310) in connection with the sympoliteia which was probably entailed by a synoikismos. Only ruins were left in the time of Pausanias (10.36.6).
ha_text: It measures 200 m (east-west) by 150 m (north-south)
relocation_details: The sympoliteia agreement between Medeon and Stiris resulted in Medeon disappearing as a polis and becoming a phratria of Stiris, probably entailed by a synoikismos. The cemetery was abandoned in C3m in connection with this sympoliteia.

chrono_text: Neon is called a polis in the urban sense at Hdt. 8.32.1 and 8.33 and, retrospectively, at Paus. 10.3.2 and 32.9, in both authors in connection with Xerxes' invasion of Phokis in 480, when Neon was burnt to the ground. The polis of Neon, demolished in 346, took the name of Tithorea when it was refounded on the same site by people returning from their temporary residence in the surrounding villages.
ha_text: The wall enclosed an area of max. c. 580 m (north-south) × 250 m (east-west).
relocation_details: The polis of Neon, demolished in 346, took the name of Tithorea when it was refounded on the same site by people returning from their temporary residence in the surrounding villages (Paus. 10.32.9).

chrono_text: Parapotamioi was never rebuilt after the dioikismos in 346, but its population was distributed between the other poleis refounded in the 330s by the Boiotians and Athenians (Paus. 10.33.8 (rC4s)). The polis ceased to exist. Pausanias' mention of an Aichmeas Parapotamios, allegedly a victor in the first Pythian Games of 586 (10.33.7).

The presumption is that Pausanias omitted the city from his list by mistake, and that it disappeared for good in 346. But, depending on the date of CID ii 5, Pedieis may have disappeared earlier, which would also explain Pausanias' silence.

Phanoteus is called a polis in the urban sense, with the political sense as a connotation, at Hdt. 8. 35. 1 in connection with Xerxes' invasion of Phokis in 480, when the town was burned to the ground. The Boiotian attack on Phanoteus in 395, as well as the dioikismos in 346, ensure that the polis belonged to the Phokian koinon in C4. Membership in C5, too, is to be assumed.
ha_text: Phanoteus is situated on a steep-sided hill measuring c. 600 m by 100 m

chrono_text: Phlygonion is recorded in Pausanias'list of poleis exposed to dioikismos in 346 (10.3.2). Pausanias' information is confirmed by the Delphic accounts, where the Phlygonians are recorded among the Phokian communities that paid the annual fine (CID ii 108.10 (322)). In literary sources the toponym is (Paus. 10.3.2 (rC4m)).
The preamble of a payment in 337/6 of the fine imposed on the Phokians in 346 lists as one of the archons of the Phokians a Kritonos Po[----]ou

Neither the toponym nor the city-ethnic is attested prior to the Heltic period; but Stiris is listed by Paus. 10 . 2 . 3 as one of the poleis exposed to dioikismos in 346, which shows that, in C4m, it was a member of the Phokian koinon.

Teithronion is called a polis in the urban sense at Hdt. 8.33 and, retrospectively, at Paus. 10.3.1 and 2, in both passages in connection with Xerxes' invasion of Phokis in 480, when the town was burned to the ground. Paus. 10.3.2-3 treats Teithronion as a polis in the urban sense in connection with the dioikismos of the Phokian cities in 346 after the Third Sacred War.

Neither the toponym nor the city-ethnic is attested prior to the Heltic period; but Trachis is listed by Paus. 10 . 2 . 3 as one of the poleis exposed to dioikismos in 346, which shows that, in C4m, it was a member of the Phokian koinon.
at 8.35, enumerating the Phokian poleis burnt down by the Persians in 480
its contribution to the fine in 322 ensures that it was a member of the Phokian koinon and undoubtedly a polis. ... in the time of Pausanias, Troneia had been incorporated into Daulis
The settlement can be traced back to C8 (Fossey (1988) 269).
ha_text: The circuit enclosed an area of c. 17 ha (Fossey (1988) 266-68; Garlan (1974) 98-112 and information from John Camp).

chrono_text: at least in the Archaic period Alalkomenai was probably a polis. Later Alalkomenai was incorporated into Koroneia.

The settlement can be traced back to the Neolithic period, and there are remains of the Geometric, Archaic and Classical periods. That its status as a polis in the political sense stems at least from the beginning of the Third Federation (i.e.from 338).
ha_text: The city walls encompassed both the hill and the harbour and enclosed an area of 30 ha (Wallace (1979) 58; Bintliff (1997a) 244).

chrono_text: The settlement can be traced back to the early Helladic period (Fossey (1988) 380).
ha_text: The acropolis walls enclose an area of c. 15 ha. The size of the lower city is unknown (Fossey (1988) 376-79); Bintliff's guesstimate is c. 23 ha ((1997a) 244).

chrono_text: The settlement can be traced back to the early Helladic period, and there are remains of the Geometric, Archaic and Classical periods. In 346 Chorsiai was destroyed by the Thebans... The epigraphical and archaeological evidence shows that the city was soon resettled.
ha_text: According to Bintliff (1997a) 244, the habitation area amounted to between 1.7 and 4.5 ha. The walls enclose residential quarters of c. 1 ha.

chrono_text: Apart from a few LH iii sherds, the settlement cannot be traced further back than the Classical period. Erythrai must have been annexed by Thebes before the synoecism of c. 431 when its population was moved to Thebes. By 395, however, Erythrai had been repopulated, but was still lying within the territory of Thebes.
relocation_details: Erythrai must have been annexed by Thebes before the synoecism of c. 431 when its population was moved to Thebes. By 395, however, Erythrai had been repopulated, but was still lying within the territory of Thebes.

chrono_text: The change of name may perhaps be connected with the refoundation of the town in c. 400 after the synoecism with Thebes (no. 221) in c. 431. Skaphai was one of the small unwalled settlements whose population was moved to Thebes in connection with the major synoecism at the outbreak of the Peloponnesian War.
relocation_details: Skaphai was one of the small unwalled settlements whose population was moved to Thebes in connection with the major synoecism at the outbreak of the Peloponnesian War (c. 431). The town may have been refounded c. 400 after the synoecism with Thebes.

chrono_text: The settlement can be traced back to the Neolithic period (Fossey (1988) 152). The attestation of the city-ethnic further suggests that Eutresis was still a polis in the Heltic period during the Third Federation.
ha_text: it enclosed an area of between 13 and 20 ha (Bintliff (1997a) 244)

chrono_text: The settlement can be traced back to the Neolithic period (Fossey (1988) 305-6). The urban centre reached its maximum size in C4 (Bintliff and Snodgrass (1988) 61-65).
ha_text: An ashlar circuit, probably of C4, encloses the lower city and covers an area of c. 42 ha (Fossey (1988) 301-5; Bintliff (1997a) 244).

chrono_text: There are no traces of habitation antedating the Archaic period (Fossey (1988) 294-95). The earliest explicit reference to Hyettos as a polis is in a military catalogue of c. 250 (SEG 26 498.3-4). But other evidence implies that Hyettos was a polis in the Archaic and Classical periods as well.
ha_text: A surface survey of the urban centre shows that it covered c. 26 ha (Bintliff (1999) 15).

chrono_text: Hysiai had been annexed by Athens, probably in 519, and was still part of Attika in 506; but in 479 Hysiai was lying in Plataian territory. Later in C5 Hysiai seems to have been a member of the sympoliteia led by Plataiai and must accordingly have been a part of Plataiis.
it can be traced back to the Neolithic period, and there are remains of the Geometric, Archaic and Classical periods
ha_text: The settlement is supposed to have covered c. 23 ha (Bintliff (1997a) 244)

chrono_text: the settlement is supposed to have covered c. 94 ha; it can be traced back to the Neolithic period, and there are remains of the Geometric, Archaic and Classical periods
ha_text: The settlement is supposed to have covered c. 94 ha (Bintliff (1997a) 244)
The size of the city is still unknown (Bintliff (1997a) 231), and so far no traces of an ancient defence circuit have been found.

chrono_text: The settlement can be traced back to EH ii and there are remains of the Archaic and Classical periods. In the Heltic and Roman periods Mykalessos was located in the territory of Tanagra.

The settlement can be traced back to the Neolithic period, and there are remains of the Geometric, Archaic and Classical periods. In 364 the city was conquered, destroyed and exposed to an andrapodismos. But the site was not abandoned. When the Thebans had been defeated at Chaironeia, however, Orchomenos was refounded, this time with Philip's support.
ha_text: The existing walls enclose an area of less than 40 ha, but the lower city must have extended further to the south, and the settlement may have covered altogether c. 90 ha

chrono_text: All attestations of Oropos as a polis are Heltic. Oropos is called a polis in the urban sense by Heracl. Cret. 7 (GGM i. 100 (C3s)) and in the political sense by Diod. 14. 17. 3 (rC4). According to the Boiotian historian Nikokrates (FGrHist 376) fr. 1, Oropos was originally a colony of Eretria.
relocation_details: The Thebans captured the city and forced its population to leave their settlement on the coast and move 7 stades inland.

chrono_text: the existence of a C4 mint strongly suggests that it must have been a polis, at least in the period after the King's Peace of 386.
The settlement can be traced back to the Neolithic period and there are remains of the Geometric, Archaic and Classical periods. In 519 Plataiai entered into an alliance with Athens.
pop_text: Six hundred Plataian hoplites fought at Plataiai in 479 (Hdt. 9. 28. 6). When the Thebans made their nocturnal attack on Plataiai in 431, the urban population must have totalled at least some 2,000 persons (Thuc. 2. 3. 2; Hansen (1997) 27-28), and there were 400 to defend the city when the siege began in 429 (Thuc. 2. 78. 3).
ha_text: Plataiai had no proper acropolis, but the oldest part of the settlement to the northwest, an area of 10 ha, was enclosed by a wall in Lesbian masonry-probably the one that was demolished in 426. (2) A new and much larger wall in polygonal masonry with interval towers enclosed an area of 80 ha. (3) Refounded in 335, the city was protected by a wall of coursed ashlar blocks enclosing an area of 70 ha

chrono_text: The reason for including Potniai in this inventory of poleis is the C6 attestation of a city-ethnic inscribed on a poros column drum dedicated to Apollo. Potniai was one of the small unwalled settlements whose population was moved to Thebes (Hell. Oxy. 20.3) in connection with the major synoecism at the outbreak of the Peloponnesian War (Demand (1990) 83-85; Moggi, Sin. 197-204 prefers a date c. 426-424). The absence of physical remains of the Heltic period (Fossey (1988) 209) may indicate that, unlike the other small towns synoecised with Thebes in 431, Potniai was not resettled.
relocation_details: Potniai was one of the small unwalled settlements whose population was moved to Thebes in connection with the major synoecism at the outbreak of the Peloponnesian War. Unlike the other small towns synoecised with Thebes in 431, Potniai was not resettled.

chrono_text: The settlement can be traced back to EH i-ii, and there are remains of the Geometric, Archaic and Classical periods.
ha_text: The circuit encloses an area of c. 3 ha, of which only a part was used for settlement.

chrono_text: Down to 479 Skolos was in Theban territory. After the Persian Wars Skolos became part of Plataian territory. But by 431 Skolos must have been annexed by Thebes once again, since it was one of the small unwalled settlements whose population was moved to Thebes in connection with the major synoecism at the outbreak of the Peloponnesian War. By 395 Skolos had been repopulated.
relocation_details: Down to c.404 probably north of the river Asopos, after 404 south of the river. By 431 its population was moved to Thebes in connection with the major synoecism. By 395 Skolos had been repopulated, but the refounded village was probably located south of the Asopos.

chrono_text: Tanagra struck silver coins on the Aiginetan standard from c. 500 to 446, and from before 410 to 374 (or later). C. 560 Tanagra joined Megara (no. 225) in colonising Herakleia Pontike (no. 715). The city-ethnic is attested in LSAG 93 and 95 no. 12 (C6).
ha_text: The city walls enclose an area of 30 ha (Roller (1987) 223).

chrono_text: In 335 Thebes was conquered by Alexander and razed to the ground. More than 6,000 men were killed, and the remaining population, some 30,000 men, women and children, were exposed to andrapodismos and sold into slavery.
pop_text: More than 6,000 men were killed, and the remaining population, some 30,000 men, women and children, were exposed to andrapodismos and sold into slavery.
ha_text: an acropolis wall around the Kadmeia with the seven famous gates enclosed an area of c. 32 ha, and a wall around the lower city enclosed an area of 350 ha
relocation_details: Shortly after the outbreak of the Peloponnesian War many small unfortified Boiotian towns were synoecised with Thebes, which thereby doubled its population. The towns merged with Thebes included Erythrai, Eteonos/Skaphai, Skolos, (Au)lis, Schoinos and Potniai.
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, bouleuterion, theatre, gymnasium, stoa

chrono_text: Thespiai was resettled and the Thespians admitted extra citizens to make up for the losses during the war. Before the autumn of 371 the city was sacked by the Thebans and depopulated. In C3e the city was resettled.
pop_text: Seven hundred Thespians were killed at Thermopylai in 480. A force of 1,800 light-armed Thespians fought in the battle of Plataiai in 479.
