ha_text: Surveys have shown that 'the early city consisted of at least four separate nuclei, spread out over a total area of about 100 ha'. The settlements covered an area of at least 50 and perhaps more than 100 ha.
relocation_details: It has been suggested that the centre of Thespiai before 480 was at the temple of Apollo, and that the settlement was moved to its Classical site only after the Persian destruction in 480. The Thespians withdrew to nearby Keressos after the sack of Thespiai before 371, and in C3e the city was resettled.

chrono_text: The earliest attestation of Thisbai as a polis is in a treaty with Chorsiai of C2e (SEG 3 342.4 = Migeotte (1984) no. 11), and the only attestation in a literary source is Paus. 9.32.2. The occurrence in C4l of a Thisban among the aphedriateuontes, however, shows that Thisbai was a polis at least from the beginning of the Third Federation (IG vii 2724 b.6 (c. 280-270)), and the reference at Hell. Oxy. 19.3 to what was probably a sympoliteia between Thespiai (no. 222), Eutresis (no. 205) and Thisbai suggests that during the First Federation Thisbai was a dependent polis dominated by Thespiai
ha_text: The circuit enclosed an area of c. 48.5 ha (Bintliff (1997a) 244).

chrono_text: Aigosthena is here listed as a polis in the urban sense in a C4 source. C3 sources use polis in the political sense about the city. This suggests that Aigosthena may in fact have been a polis by C4, as indicated by Ps.-Skylax. However, an inscription of c. 300 describes Aigosthena as a kome of Megara, so the city may have had a double status as both a kome and a polis; alternatively, the city developed into a polis only in the Hellenistic period.
Synoecism appears to have occurred in C8 through the union of five komai, including the kome of Megara (Plut. Quaest. Graec. 17; cf. Moggi, Sin. 29-34).
pop_text: Megara's estimated peak population (C5) was 40,000, including 15,000 slaves and free non-citizens (Legon (1981) 23-24, 167-68, 258-59, 268-71).
ha_text: The town centres on the twin hills of Karia and Alkathoa, both under 300 m, which are encircled by a 3.5 km perimeter wall (of C4?) enclosing an area of 140 ha (Alexandris (1970)).
relocation_details: Synoecism appears to have occurred in C8 through the union of five komai, including the kome of Megara (Plut. Quaest. Graec. 17; cf. Moggi, Sin. 29-34).

chrono_text: That it may already have been a polis by C4 is suggested by Ps.-Skylax 39. Several sources indicate that Pagai was a polis in the political sense in the Heltic period.

Based on a king list (Diod. 7.9), however, Bacchis may have been the actual founder, c. 900. Legend attributed the Corinthian synoecism to King Aletes at the time of the foundation. Salmon (1984) 57-58 concludes that Corinthia was unified from the time of the Dorian conquest, c. 900
pop_text: The estimated C5 population is 70,000, including slaves and any non-citizens (Salmon (1984) 165-69).
ha_text: The Classical city walls of Corinth were approximately 7.5 km in length, enclosing an urban area of 600-700 ha (Carpenter in Bon et al. (1936) 1-83).
relocation_details: Legend attributed the Corinthian synoecism to King Aletes at the time of the foundation. Salmon (1984) 57-58 concludes that Corinthia was unified from the time of the Dorian conquest, c. 900.
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, bouleuterion, theatre, stoa

pop_text: The free population in C5 and C4 is estimated at 20,000 (Beloch (1922) 276).
ha_text: The city walls enclosed an area of approximately 175 ha
relocation_details: In C4l, the city was relocated by Demeterios Poliorketes: the outer city wall was demolished, the lower acropolis wall was strengthened (2.5 m thickness), and a new wall was built surrounding the upper acropolis. The urban population moved from the old city on the plain below the acropolis to what had been the lower acropolis.
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, bouleuterion, prytaneion, theatre, stoa

chrono_text: The appearance of coinage c. 500 should probably be seen as a strong indication that Aigai was already a polis in the political sense by this date. Aigai's inclusion in Ps.-Skylax's list of Achaian poleis (42)-a section which should probably be dated to the years around 370, and it may not be coincidental that coinage issues cease at about the same time. According to Paus. 7.25.12 and 8.15.9, Aigai was abandoned due to 'weakness', and its inhabitants incorporated within the polis of Aigeira.
relocation_details: Aigai was abandoned due to 'weakness', and its inhabitants incorporated within the polis of Aigeira (no. 230), where they were nevertheless permitted to retain their own ethnic.

chrono_text: Classified by Hdt 1.145 as one of the original twelve Achaian mere; the internal use of the collective city-ethnic on coinage from c. 330 would indicate that Aigeira was by this period also a polis in the political sense; Polyb. 2.41.7-8 (rC4) calls it a polis in the political sense and attests to its membership of the Achaian Confederacy prior to the time of Philip II and Alexander.
ha_text: a separately acropolis wall enclosing 3.5 ha
relocation_details: A synoecism is suggested: the name Hyperesia was originally applied to the acropolis area, but after synoecism the epineion of Aigeira gave its name to the whole polis. The population of Aigeira expanded again when the polis incorporated the inhabitants of Aigai.

chrono_text: The external collective use of the city-ethnic is attested in a supposedly C7 Delphic oracle cited by Ion of Chios, but it is debatable whether Aigion's status as a polis in the political sense can really be retrojected as far back as C7. Aigion was one of the original twelve Achaian mere (Hdt. 1.145).
relocation_details: Strabo says that Aigion was synoecised from seven or eight demoi (8.3.2; Moggi, Sin. 125-27).

chrono_text: suggests that it was a polis in C4
archaeological record from Kastro which appears to indicate virtual continuity from the late Archaic/early Classical period through until Roman times
relocation_details: Boura was affected severely by the earthquake of 373 which destroyed Helike. Pausanias' comment that Boura was swiftly refounded by inhabitants who had been away on military service during the earthquake.

chrono_text: This should indicate that Dyme was a synoecised polis by 460. Hdt. 1.145 lists it among the original twelve mere of Achaia. the name of Dyme appears to be familiar already to Hecat. fr. 121 (c. 500). Koerner (1974) 469 argues that Dyme must have been synoecised prior to 496.
relocation_details: Strabo says that Dyme was synoecised from eight demoi (8.3.2), and there are other indications in generally late sources that the toponym was adopted relatively late. It is possible that Paleia and Stratos were the names of two of the demoi that were synoecised, along with the polichne of Teuthea. This should indicate that Dyme was a synoecised polis by 460.

chrono_text: The city, which was overwhelmed by a tidal wave occasioned by an earthquake in 373. The early physical existence of Helike is attested in Hom. Il. 2.575 and in a C5e inscription (SEG 36 718).

Polyb. 2.41.7-8 states that it was one of the poleis (in a political sense) that formed the Achaian Confederacy prior to the time of Philip II and Alexander. It was certainly a polis by C3, when it began to strike Achaian Confederate coinage. Its precise status in earlier times is unsure, though Paus. 7.25.3-5 describes it as a polisma and describes how it owed its strength to taking in refugees after the destruction of Mykenai (no. 353) in C5m.

Polyb. 2.41.7-8 records it as one of the poleis (in the political sense) that constituted the Achaian Confederacy in the period prior to Philip II and Alexander. The external use of individual and collective city-ethnics suggests strongly that it was a polis in the political sense by the end of C3, though this dates to after its refoundation by Antigonos Gonatas.
relocation_details: Refoundation by Antigonos Gonatas (Strabo 8.7.5).

chrono_text: Olenos was abandoned due to 'weakness' (Paus. 7.18.1), and its inhabitants were incorporated within Dyme (no. 234) (Strabo 8.7.1). Demand (1990) 61-64 argues that this must have taken place after the time at which Herodotos was writing, though Strabo 8.7.1 seems to imply that Olenos still existed by 280; it had certainly been abandoned by C2 (Polyb. 2.41.7).

Patrai is listed by Hdt. 1.145 as one of the original twelve mere of Achaia and is described as a polis (in the urban sense) by Ps.-Skylax 42 (C4f). Moggi, Sin. 89-95 dates this latter synoecism to C6l or C5e.
relocation_details: According to tradition, Patrai was originally formed by the synoecism of Aroe, Antheia and Mesatis (Paus. 7.18.2-6). In 279, Patrai underwent a dioikismos, and its inhabitants settled in the polismata of Mesatis, Antheia, Boline, Argyra and Arba as well as Patrai (Paus. 7.18.6). Curtius suggested that these polismata had formerly been demoi, which had earlier been incorporated through a second process of synoecism. Moggi dates this latter synoecism to C6l or C5e.

chrono_text: According to tradition, Pellene was exposed to andrapodismos and destroyed by Sikyon in C6e, but refounded shortly afterwards. At the outbreak of the Peloponnesian War in 431, Pellene was the first Achaian city to join the Peloponnesian League.
relocation_details: The fact that a homonymous kome of Pellene existed between Pellene and Aigion may suggest that the polis of Pellene was the result of a series of synoecisms to which one of its constituents gave its name. Other possible participants in this synoecism are Mysaion, Kyros, the harbour of Aristonautai, Poseidion, and Olouros.

chrono_text: Designated by Hdt. 1.145 as one of the original twelve mere of Achaia. Polyb. 2.41.7-8 states that Pharai was one of the Achaian poleis (in the political sense) that constituted the Achaian Confederacy prior to the time of Philip II and Alexander.

Paus. 7.26.10 calls Phelloe a polisma and claims that it had been settled ever since the Ionian occupation of Achaia. surface exploration and limited excavation in the area have revealed settlement evidence and burials dating from C8s and continuing into the Archaic and Classical periods

An even earlier attestation of what appears to be the external individual city-ethnic in an Archaic inscription (SGDI 1644) may just possibly indicate that Rhypes was a polis in the political sense during C6. Rhypes is one of the original twelve mere of Achaia (Hdt 1.145). it would appear that it persisted until its destruction by Augustus, when its territory was distributed between Aigion (no. 231) and Pharai (no. 241) (Strabo 8.7.5), as well as Patrai (no. 239) (Paus. 7.18.7).

Tritaia is described as one of the original twelve mere of Achaia by Hdt 1.145. Polyb. 2.41.7-8 (rC4) calls it a polis in the political sense. Tritaia was eventually annexed by Augustus, and its territory assigned to Patrai.

described by Strabo as a chora around Amphidolia (no. 247) with a monthly market and previously a polis of Pisatis. In C5l or C4e the Alasyeis and the Akroreioi made a joint inscribed dedication at Olympia

It is described retrospectively as a polis (in the political sense) of the Akroreians c. 400 (Diod. 14. 17. 8); Xen. Hell. 7. 4. 14 refers collectively to the poleis of the Akroreians in 365, without naming Alion.

In the years following c. 400 it is seen to function as a polis: it is described as a (perioikic) polis in the political sense at Xen. Hell. 3.2.23 and 30, and the Amphidoloi made a dedication at Olympia in C5 or C4e (Siewert (1991) no. 2)
known only from IvO 10 (c. 475-450), where the collective and external use is attested in a 50-year treaty of friendship with the equally unknown Metapioi
The city-ethnic is Χαλάδριος known only from IvO 11 = Minon (1994) no. 9, an agreement of C5e

Paus. 6. 22. 4 reports that it was a perioikic community, though presumably not Pisatan since it joined Pisa in revolt against Elis c. 570, and was destroyed. Strabo 8. 3. 32 says that Dyspontion was destroyed and that most of its inhabitants emigrated to Epidamnos (no. 79) and Apollonia (no. 77). There is no evidence of its later existence.
In 471 (Diod. 11 . 54 . 1 ), 'after the Persian Wars' (Strabo 8 . 3 . 2 ), the Eleians were synoecised. A unitary Eleian state certainly existed before 471 , as did the town Elis.
pop_text: There is very limited evidence on population size: 3 , 000 hoplites from Elis are recorded in 418 (Thuc. 5 . 58 . 1 , 75 . 5 ) and 3 , 000 jointly from Elis, Triphylia, Akroreia and Lasion in 394 (Xen. Hell . 4 . 2 . 16 ).
ha_text: On a plan of settlement in the town of Elis from the Classical to the Roman periods the area of settlement is shown as between 4 and 5 km², extending north of the river Peneios
relocation_details: In 471 the Eleians were synoecised. Diodorus says the Eleians were synoecised from numerous small poleis into one; Strabo that they had previously lived in villages, and then came together into the polis Elis from many demoi.
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, bouleuterion, prytaneion, theatre, gymnasium, law_courts, stoa

chrono_text: It is described as a polis (in the political sense) of the Akroreians c. 400 (Diod. 14. 17. 8); Xen. Hell. 7. 4. 14 refers collectively to the poleis of the Akroreians in 365
The community is known only from IvO 9 of c. 500, a 100-year alliance with Elis (no. 251).
There was a town on that site at least from C5 to the Roman imperial period

The external collective city-ethnic is used on a C6s dedication at Olympia. Paus. 6.22.8 says that it was originally a polisma, but in his day had only a few buildings with a temple and statue of Artemis Alpheiaia.
When Marganeis became perioikic is not known: it was certainly perioikic by the Eleian-Spartan war of c. 400, after which it became independent. In Strabo's day it was part of Amphidolia.
known only from IvO 10 (c. 475-450), where the collective and external use is attested in a 50-year treaty of friendship with the equally unknown Anaitoi
It is described as a polis (in a political sense) of the Akroreians c. 400 (Diod. 14. 17. 8)

Pisa later broke away from Eleian control, and again formed an independent, or at least selfgoverning, state 365-c. 362, but was then reabsorbed into the Eleian state. It merits inclusion in this Inventory primarily because it was quite possibly a polis in 365-c. 362. It is not heard of again after the battle of Mantinea in 362, and presumably rapidly returned to Elis.

The excavation has shown a significant Classical nucleated settlement from c. 425, but without monumental architecture of any kind. The archaeological findings are compatible with destruction in the 360s.
Head, HN² 446 dates the first coinage of Alea to c. 430; Babelon, Traité ii.3.630 proposes 421.
ha_text: The acropolis measured 14.3 ha, and on it was a building which Meyer identified as a military barracks. The city below the 'Stadtberg' occupied 14.6 ha; no substantial remains are extant, but scattered remains seem to be of houses.

chrono_text: Alipheira is well attested only in Heltic sources, where it is called a polis both in the urban sense (Polyb. 4.78.11 (r 219)) and in the political sense (SEG 25 447.9 (C3)). Since Alipheira certainly existed before C3 (infra), the city may possibly have been a polis. votives go back to C6m
relocation_details: According to Paus. 8.26.5, a part of the population actually relocated to Megalopolis, but the city was not abandoned. According to Paus. 8.27.4, Alipheira was one of the cities that the Arkadian Confederacy had voted to incorporate into Megalopolis.

chrono_text: Strabo 8.3.12 classifies Asea as a kome tes Megalopolitidos, but the reference is to C1. According to Paus. 8.27.3, Asea was among the communities that the Arkadian Confederacy voted to relocate to Megalopolis; however, it is not known whether this relocation was ever wholly or partly carried out.
pop_text: Forsén and Forsén (1997) 176 estimate that the urban population could have been max. 2,000-3,000. The total population of the polis is estimated at 2,500-3,500 at the most.
ha_text: Forsén and Forsén (1997) 166-67 and Forsén et al. (2002) 100-2 report that they found Archaic and Classical pottery around the acropolis, and that the area yielding finds was at least 13 ha, but perhaps twice as large (the eastern area of the site having been covered with alluvium brought down by the river Alpheios).
relocation_details: According to Paus. 8.27.3, Asea was among the communities that the Arkadian Confederacy voted to relocate to Megalopolis; however, it is not known whether this relocation was ever wholly or partly carried out.

chrono_text: At 8.27.3 (r 371) Pausanias retrospectively lists Dipaia as a polis, and at 3.11.7 (rC5f) it is classified as a polisma.
relocation_details: According to Paus. 8.27.3 (r 371), Dipaia was among the communities that the Arkadian Confederacy voted to relocate to Megalopolis; however, it is unknown whether this decision was implemented.

chrono_text: The treaty IPArk no. 15.2 describes itself as an agreement of synoikia and provides for the inclusion of the Euaiminians into the Orchomenian citizen body; however, Euaimon probably continued to exist
relocation_details: The treaty IPArk no. 15.2 describes itself as an agreement of synoikia and provides for the inclusion of the Euaiminians into the Orchomenian citizen body; however, Euaimon probably continued to exist and may thereafter have been a dependent polis of Orchomenos.

According to Paus. 8.27.3, Eutaia was among the communities that the Arkadian Confederacy voted to relocate to Megalopolis. It is unknown whether the relocation was ever carried out: the site seems not to have been abandoned, since coins of the Arkadian and Achaian Confederacies have been found there.

chrono_text: The only source that classifies Gortys as a polis is Paus. 8.27.4 (r 371) and 28.1, but the external collective use of the city-ethnic is found on a C5f dedication of spoils at Delphi (Syll.³ 49). The ethnic is attested as a personal name already in C6.

the entry in the Delphic catalogue of theorodokoi of C5l-C4e (ed. by Daux in REG 62 (1949) p. 6 l. 10) may refer to this site; sherds and tiles from the walled area date to the Classical and Heltic periods.
ha_text: The wall runs for c. 640 m and encloses an area of 1.7 ha

chrono_text: Helisson is called polis in the urban sense by SEG 37 340.6-7 (C4f) and Diod. 16.39.5 (r 352), and in the political sense by SEG 37 340.5 (C4f). the city still existed in 352, when it was captured and pillaged by the Spartans (Diod. 16.39.5).
relocation_details: According to Paus. 8.27.3 (r 371), Helisson was among the communities that the Arkadian Confederacy voted to relocate to Megalopolis; it is not known, however, whether this decision was implemented, and the city still existed in 352.

chrono_text: Heraian coinage began c. 510; the collective use of the city-ethnic is found externally in SEG 11 1045 (c. 500)
pop_text: Bölte (1913b) estimated the citizen population at min. 2,000.
ha_text: The city of Heraia seems to have occupied a large area (Jost (1985) 73), but since the site has not been systematically investigated, no description of the urban layout is possible
relocation_details: According to Strabo 8.3.2, Heraia was synoecised (at an unknown date) from nine demoi. These demes were perhaps minor settlements in the territory.

chrono_text: The city-ethnic is found externally in the collective use in F.Delphes iii.4 191 (C5f), and in the individual use in IG ii² 66 c (c. 400) and CID ii 12.i.2 (341/40). The earliest source that classifies Kaphy(i)ai as a polis is Staatsverträge 476.25, 92 (c. 265).

ha_text: The Heltic fortification wall is 2,500 m long and encloses an area of c. 58 ha (Petritaki (1996) 83-84).

chrono_text: the toponym is Koila in REG 62 (1949) p. 6 ll. 4-5 (C5l-C4e)

since there is a C4 occurrence of the city-ethnic in the external individual use (Aristox. fr. 135, Wehrli), the city must have existed in the Classical period and was possibly a polis (Nielsen (1996a) 131-32), as it certainly was in C3

Lousoi may be called a polis in a treaty dating to 500-475 (Nomima i no. 57, but cf. Dubois (1986) 220); a series of decrees dating to C4l onwards (IG v.2 388-96) frequently refers to the city as a polis in the political sense.
pop_text: Stangl (1999b) estimates that the territory could support max. 1,000 individuals.
ha_text: The acropolis of Lousoi is crowned by a C4 fortification wall, possibly of mudbrick on a polygonal stone foundation, and encloses an area of 0.3 ha

chrono_text: No Archaic or Classical source mentions Lykosoura. At 8.27.4 (r 371), Pausanias states that Lykosoura was among the communities that the Arkadian Confederacy voted to relocate to Megalopolis; however, he goes on to explain that this decision was not implemented (8.27.6), because the city resisted the relocation. Since the very first source that mentions Lykosoura (SEG 41 332 (c. 230-190)) describes it as a polis (in the political sense, e.g. l. 3) and refers to the politai (ll. 7-8), it is possible that at the foundation of Megalopolis the city was left as a dependent polis inside Megalopolitan territory.

The town of Mantinea (the date of whose foundation is uncertain; see Hodkinson and Hodkinson (1981) 256-61). According to Strabo 8.3.2, Mantinea was originally synoecised from five demoi. The synoecism cannot be dated. In 385, the Spartans undid the synoecism by dioikismos. However, in 370 the city was synoecised again.
pop_text: At Thermopylai Mantinea contributed 500 hoplites (Hdt. 7.202), and in 420, 1,000 soldiers helped to protect the Olympic Games against an anticipated Spartan attack. According to Lys. 34.7, the Mantineans did not number 3,000; it is not possible to give any exact estimate of the size of population on this basis.
ha_text: It encloses an area of 124 ha (Hodkinson and Hodkinson (1981) 256) and runs for 3,942 m with ten gates and 126 towers.
relocation_details: Mantinea was originally synoecised from five demoi (komai). In 385, the Spartans undid the synoecism by dioikismos, dispersing the Mantineans to the old villages. In 370 the city was synoecised again.

chrono_text: Diod. 15.72.4, who gives a compressed account of an event and dates the synoecism to 368; and from Paus. 8.27.1-8, who gives a detailed account of the original plan for the foundation as well as an account of the implementation of this plan, and dates the synoecism to shortly after Leuktra. the decision to synoecise may have been taken shortly after Leuktra (Paus.) but the implementation may have been postponed to 368 (Diod.).
pop_text: Diod. 18.70.1 states that in 318 the city managed to put 15,000 men under arms, but this figure includes metics and slaves (Bury (1898) 16); Gardner et al. (1893) 3 supposes a total population of 65,000. Even on the most cautious estimate-that only 100 ha was occupied by habitation and that each ha accommodated only 100 people-the urban population of the city would have been 10,000.
ha_text: The area enclosed by walls measured c. 350 ha; this area, however, includes the river Helisson, which divided the city in two.
relocation_details: Megalopolis was created by synoecism of the Arkadian Confederacy, involving twenty komai belonging to the Parrhasians and Mainalians relocated to a new site, planned by a board of ten oikistai. The synoecism was planned shortly after Leuktra and implemented in 368.
monuments: agora, temple, bouleuterion, ekklesiastierion, theatre, stoa

chrono_text: at Petrovouni, 3 km west of the city, a sanctuary has been found; the temple is Heltic, but architectural fragments and votives go back to C7. The oldest find at the site is an Archaic statuette of Zeus.
ha_text: The wall encloses an area of 11 ha (von Gaertringen and Lattermann (1911) 31).
relocation_details: According to Paus. 8.27.4, Methydrion was among the communities that the Arkadian Confederacy voted to relocate to Megalopolis. However, it is uncertain whether Megalopolis in the Classical period effectively controlled the city, which was clearly not abandoned.

chrono_text: In C4m, Nestane may have been a dependent polis inside Mantinean territory
At the presumed site of Nonakris, Archaic and Classical pottery has been found, together with some poor architectural fragments.

pop_text: The only indication we have of the order of size of the Orchomenian population is the fact that the city fielded 600 hoplites at Plataiai in 479
ha_text: The fortification wall encloses the upper part of the Kalpaki hill; it was 2,300 m long and enclosed an area of c. 20 ha.
relocation_details: Pausanias at 8.13.2 explicitly states that the earlier city was on the Kalpaki hill, but that in his day habitation was under the circuit wall. The inference is that in C4 the city was moved from the southern slope to the hill when the wall was constructed (so Winter (1989) 194-95), although habitation in the former town seems not to have ceased.

chrono_text: According to Paus. 8.27.3, Oresthasion was one of the communities that the Arkadian Confederacy voted to relocate to Megalopolis (no. 282). It is likely that the city came under Megalopolitan control, but it was probably not abandoned since a surface survey has brought to light numerous Heltic sherds at the probable site of the city.
ha_text: According to Pikoulas (1988) 102, the megalos archaios oikismos of Oresthasion covered an area of 450 × 200 m.
relocation_details: According to Paus. 8.27.3, Oresthasion was one of the communities that the Arkadian Confederacy voted to relocate to Megalopolis (no. 282). It is likely that the city came under Megalopolitan control, but it was probably not abandoned.

chrono_text: C. 230-210 a Delphic theorodokos resided in the city. The city is called polis at Hdt. 6.127.3.
ha_text: The extent of the city is not stated by any authority, but there was probably occupation both inside and outside the wall. Papandreou seems to believe that the site was of considerable size.

chrono_text: it may have had a Delphic theorodokos in C5l-C4e

pop_text: Stangl (1999a), (1999b), estimates that the territory could support max. 5,000 individuals.
ha_text: A recent survey suggests that the urban centre occupied c. 20 ha (Stangl (1999b) 178).

chrono_text: At Bassai, on the southern slope of Mt. Kotilion, the sanctuary of Apollo Bassitas or Epikourios can be traced back to c. C8s or c. 700. Citizens of Phigaleia achieved Olympic victories in C6.
ha_text: 'Fortifications embrace a large area, approximately 1.5 km north-south and 1.3 km east-west'

chrono_text: SEG 24 299 is a C6s dedication of a shield by the city at Olympia, indicating that it was conducting military campaigns by that time. For Archaic stray finds from the site, see Jost (1985) 57. Polybios at 4.70.3 calls the city a palaion Arkadon ktisma.
ha_text: The circuit wall enclosed an area of 80 ha (Meyer (1959) 1424).

chrono_text: the ethnic Parpylaios (SEG 18 157 (C5))

sufficient Classical pottery has now been found to push the existence of the town back into C5 (H.Williams et al. (1997) 41, 43; part of the road system may also predate C4: ibid. 42). So far, the earliest pottery from the site is Late Archaic (ibid. 8). The toponym is in SEG 35 371 (C5f).
ha_text: it seems to have enclosed an area of at least 40 ha (calculated on the basis of H. Williams (1983) fig. 1)
relocation_details: According to Paus. 8.22.1-2, old Stymphalos was not in the same place as the known city, but we have no idea about where this can have been; however, at the village of Lafka, part of a well-preserved polygonal circuit wall has been found, thus providing a possible candidate for early Stymphalos. The later city was laid out as an orthogonally planned town some time in the fourth century (= 375-350).

pop_text: Tegea fielded 500 hoplites for Thermopylai (Hdt. 7. 202), but at Plataiai the city provided 1,500 (Hdt. 9. 27. 3) in addition to 1,500 psiloi (Hdt. 9. 61. 2); at the battle of Nemea in 394 the city may have fielded upwards of 2,400 hoplites (Roy (1971b) 440). Finally, the stasis of 370 sent 800 oligarchs into exile at Sparta (Xen. Hell. 6. 5. 10). These figures suggest a substantial population, but its exact size cannot be estimated (cf. Forsen (2000) 36-39), and it is, furthermore, not known how large a fraction of the population resided in the town.
ha_text: The area enclosed by the wall seems to have had the shape of 'an irregular ellipse, with the long axis measuring 2,000 metres and the short axis 1,400 metres' (Voyatzis (1990) 12; cf. Berard (1892) 547). It measured max. c. 190 ha.
relocation_details: According to Strabo 8.3.2, Tegea was (at an uncertain date) synoecised from nine demes, whose names are given at Paus. 8.45.1.
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, theatre, law_courts, stoa

chrono_text: Pausanias states that it was formerly a polisma. According to Paus. 8.27.4, Teuthis was, prior to the foundation of Megalopolis (r 371), among the communities whose relationship to Orchomenos could be described by the phrase syntelein es.
ha_text: The city proper lay below the acropolis. The site is now occupied by the village of Dimitsana, and so the degree of ancient urbanisation cannot be estimated.

chrono_text: the sherds at the site range from Classical to Heltic, but Jost (1985) 44 refers to earlier material.

ha_text: The city was situated on a hill and covered an extensive area on the lower slopes, not all of which was occupied

chrono_text: Thisoa is listed as a polis in the urban or political sense at Paus. 8.27.4 (r 371). The oldest remains reported are C3.

There are clear indications of habitation at Torthyneion, with abundant sherds ranging from the Geometric to the Heltic periods. Syll.³ 90.9 = REG 62 (1949) 4-12 (C5l-C4e) attests the existence of a Delphic theorodokos at Torthyneion.

According to Paus. 8.27.4, Trapezous was among the communities that the Arkadian Confederacy decreed to relocate to Megalopolis; however, the Trapezountioi resisted the decision, but a part of the population was relocated by use of force, whereas another part left the Peloponnese (Paus. 8.27.5). The synoecism thus meant the end of the city's existence.
relocation_details: Trapezous was among the communities that the Arkadian Confederacy decreed to relocate to Megalopolis; however, the Trapezountioi resisted the decision, but a part of the population was relocated by use of force, whereas another part left the Peloponnese. The synoecism thus meant the end of the city's existence.

chrono_text: From Hdt. 4.148.4 and Xen. Hell. 3.2.23, 31 it appears that in C5s (at the latest) Ep(e)ion was a perioikic community of Elis
ha_text: The acropolis covers an area of 150 × 25 m.

chrono_text: It appears from a combination of Xen. Hell. 3.2.20, 25 and 30 that by C5l (at the latest) Epitalion was a perioikic community of Elis

pop_text: Lepreon provided 200 hoplites for the battle of Plataiai in 479 (Hdt. 9. 28. 4), but this is the only indication we have for the order of size of the population.
ha_text: The extent of the lower city was considerable, according to Frazer (1898) iii. 474 (cf. Pritchett (1989) 59).
relocation_details: According to Strabo 8.3.30, Lepreon at some point absorbed Pylos by synoecism (Moggi, Sin. 168 proposes a date c. 460; cf. Demand (1990) 64).

chrono_text: a Doric peripteral temple, probably of C5e (Trianti (1985) 23-24); After c. 400-369 (SEG 35 389) the city is mentioned as existing only once (AR (1996-97) 49 (C3)), and it is absent from Polybios' list of Triphylian cities at 4.77.9; it may have ceased to exist by 219, the date to which Polybios' description of Triphylia refers.

In C5m Phrixa(i) seems to have been considered to be Arkadian (Pherekydes (FGrHist 3) fr. 161), but from a combination of Hdt. 4.148.4 and Xen. Hell. 3.2.23, 30 it appears that in C5l (at the latest) the city was a perioikic community of Elis

In C5 Pyrgos was probably the victim of Eleian aggression (Hdt. 4. 148. 4), but apart from that nothing is known about the political history of the city in the Archaic and Classical periods; it may be assumed, however, that it became a perioikic dependency of Elis... was liberated c. 400, joined the Triphylian Federation and through it the Arkadian Confederacy. Here a small C5e temple of Zeus has been found... a city of C5s with traces of earlier occupation.
In 371, the Eleians claimed that Skillous was a polis belonging to them, but the implications of this claim are uncertain. After 371, Skillous disappears from the records.
Malkin (1994) 85 tentatively advances Thouria (no. 322) and Aithaia as possible C8 foundations.

The earliest find is an early Archaic Protocorinthian sherd. Asine was peopled with the expelled population of Argolic Asine (Dryopians) when it was destroyed after the First Messenian War. After 338/7 it may have belonged to a Messenian koinon.

Aulon is called a polis only by Steph.Byz. 147.6, who lists it as 'one of the 100 poleis', suggesting a Classical or Heltic source such as Theopompos; it is not clear whether polis is here used in the urban or the political sense. It is not certain, however, that Aulon was a polis in the Classical period.
ha_text: Hope Simpson (1972) 94 and n. 101, however, suggests MME no. 602, Ag. Ilias, a Classical and Heltic site of at least 3 ha with a good view over the coastal route.

chrono_text: Archaic to mediaeval remains, as well as prehistoric. It is generally thought to have been a perioikic polis during Spartan rule over Messenia.

Pausanias says that it was originally called Aipeia, but was renamed Koroneia by its Theban oecist, Epimelides, after the liberation of Messenia... This suggests a re foundation c. 369; however, Lazenby (1972) 89 and Hope Simpson (1972) 99 n. 125 suggest that Korone (and Kolonides) were founded in the aftermath of 369 to isolate perioikic Asine and Mothone.
relocation_details: Pausanias says it was originally called Aipeia, but was renamed Koroneia by its Theban oecist Epimelides after the liberation of Messenia, suggesting a re-foundation c. 369.

chrono_text: the decree IG v.1 1421 (C4-C3) demonstrates that it was a polis in the political sense long before that date; parts are C5 or later; some black-figured pottery; In 365/4, when Kyparissos was captured by Arkadian forces, it was evidently a defended settlement
ha_text: a settlement of 1 ha or more

chrono_text: Messene was founded after the first Boiotian invasion of Laconia by Epameinondas and his allies in 370/69
ha_text: They are c. 8 km long and enclose c. 290 ha (Meyer (1978) 138), not all of which was built up.

chrono_text: Mothone is called a polis in the urban sense by Thuc. 2.25.2; Hope Simpson (1972) 98 n. 96 notes evidence of late Archaic habitation. Pausanias says that Mothone was founded by exiles from Nauplia.

Pharai was clearly a polis by 182, when it joined the Achaian Confederacy separately from Messene (Polyb. 23. 17. 2); but earlier references are vague about its status. Archaeological evidence suggests that it was a significant settlement from early times, and a fortified polis at least post-369.
where Archaic to Roman finds have been made, including a C7 (?) terracotta statuette

Thouria was the only perioikic polity apart from Aithaia (no. 312) to revolt in 465 (Thuc. 1.101.2). It is not explicitly called a polis, however, before IvO 46 (a C2m boundary adjudication with Megalopolis (no. 282)), where it is called polis in the political sense. But it was probably a polis long before.
ha_text: the extent of the historical site is not stated in the literature

chrono_text: Paus. 3.2.5 (rC8e) gives an anachronistic version of history in which Aigys, already a perioikic polis, was enslaved by the Lakedaimonians under Archelaos (c. 775 - c. 760). If Aigys existed as a named settlement from late Classical to Roman times, it was probably Tsamaina Kamaras in the south of Megalopolitan territory, which has C4l-second century ad pottery. We are left with no certain evidence that Aigys was ever a perioikic polis, but it was a settlement after C4m and perhaps in early times.

Thuc. 5.41.2 places Anthana (with Thyrea) in Kynouria, and says it was a Lakedaimonian possession in C5l; It will have remained Spartan until 338, when Philip II assigned some Spartan territory to Argos

Aphroditia may still have existed in C4m or later (see Boia (no. 327)). It is not attested after C4 (except in Paus. 3.22.11), and if it disappeared as a polis by c. 300, both cult and legend should predate that time.
relocation_details: Aphroditia was synoecised into Boia along with Etis and Side, presumably in C4s (Paus. 3.22.11).

chrono_text: Geometric to Archaic finds in the area tend to push the existence of this possible polis back before 300. Belbina is called a polis in the (urban and) political sense only by Paus. 8.27.4 (r 369), in his list of poleis from which Megalopolis (no. 282) was founded.
relocation_details: Belbina is listed among the poleis from which Megalopolis was founded via synoecism (r 369), as stated in Paus. 8.27.4.

chrono_text: Supposing Boia to have been founded in C4m (supra), it certainly absorbed the territory of Etis. Possibly these poleis or settlements synoecised with an existing polis of Boia in the late Classical period, perhaps as a result of changes in the Spartan state following 369.
relocation_details: According to Pausanias, Boia was founded by Boios the Heraklid, who brought men from three poleis, Etis, Aphrodisia and Side. Possibly these poleis or settlements synoecised with an existing polis of Boia in the late Classical period, perhaps as a result of changes in the Spartan state following 369.

chrono_text: According to Paus. 3.23.6-7, Epidauros Limera was founded by envoys from the Asklepieion of Argolic Epidauros. If true, this would imply a date after c. 500.
ha_text: The fortified area enclosed c. 3 ha; the lower town was probably much more extensive

chrono_text: It may have disappeared in C4m. Paus. 3.22.13 (if correctly restored) describes Etis as in ruins.
relocation_details: Etis, with Aphroditia and Side, were synoecised into Boia, presumably in C4s.

chrono_text: A city-ethnic may be attested as early as c. 500 in the form... it has a long life, though the archaeological evidence suggests late Classical and Heltic decline.

If the name lists IG v.1 1133-34 (infra) are public records of athletic victories by people (citizens) of Geronthrai (Hodkinson (1999) 156-57), then it was possibly a polis by c. 500. It was probably lost to Sparta in 195.
Archaeological finds, however, begin in the Archaic period. SEG 11 926 (C5) is a bronze goat dedicated to Apollo-ineios (Karneios?).

The pottery (C5m-C4) suggests a Classical foundation (Pikoulas (1988a) 161-64; AR 29 (1982-83) 28-29; BCH 107 (1983) 764-67), possibly by Lakedaimonians. The finds are consistent with abandonment during or after C4.
relocation_details: Now or earlier, the town was synoecised into Megalopolis (no. 282) (Paus. 8.27.4); it was ruined but 'not quite vanished' in Pausanias' day (8.34.6).

chrono_text: the appearance of one of its citizens in a catalogue of proxenoi from Keos (IG xii.5 542.21, Karthaia (no. 492)? (C4m)) proves that it was a polity already in the Classical period.
Thuc. 4. 54. 1, the earliest evidence for the town; IG ii² 9110, C4e
The acropolis has produced Protogeometric to Roman finds, including a late Archaic or early Classical votive stele. The earliest mention is at Hom. Il. 2.585.
the bronze plaque SEG 13 239 (LSAG 169 no. 22; BCH 77 (1953) 395 ff; Argos, c. 475?), the earliest evidence for Oinous
If correctly identified, Oios may have existed only in the Classical period.
The archaeological evidence to date is too imprecise to confirm that Oitylos was a Classical polis, though that remains a possibility.

Pellana is called a polis only retrospectively, by Diod. 15. 67. 2 bis (r 369/8). For Paus. 3. 21. 2 Pellana was a polis 'of old'. The only Classical attestation of the city-ethnic is individual and external, in IG xii. 5 542. 20-1 (Karthaia (no. 492)? (C4m)), which lists a man of Pellana as proxenos of the issuing polis and provides confirmation that it was a polity in C4m.
Finds from c. C7l to Roman are evidence of a nucleated settlement.
From archaeological evidence, Sellasia was probably founded in C6m and abandoned in C3 (Catling (2002) 238-40). It was seen in ruins by Pausanias (3.10.7).

relocation_details: Pausanias says that Side was one of three poleis from which Boia was synoecised in early times.

pop_text: Referring to 480, Hdt. 7.234.2 makes Damaratos say that the polis of Sparta contains approximately 8,000 men-possibly the only remotely reliable estimate of total Spartiate numbers. In 480, the total population in citizen families may have been c. 35,000-40,000 (Cartledge (1979) 222).
ha_text: expanded into walls with a circuit of 48 stades (Polyb. 9.26a.2), enclosing c. 209 ha (Cartledge and Spawforth (1989) 133).

pop_text: at Nemea in 394, the Argives could muster c. 7,000 hoplites (Xen. Hell. 4.2.17), to whom should be added a contingent of knights (Charneux (1991) 316 n. 126). A total of perhaps 7,500 hoplites and knights in the field army corresponds to some 12,000 adult male citizens of hoplite status (Hansen (1985) 11-13, 16-21).
ha_text: In the Classical period the walls seem to have enclosed an area of over 200 ha (Lang (1996) 174 and Abb. 34).
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, bouleuterion, prytaneion, ekklesiastierion, theatre, gymnasium, law_courts, stoa

pop_text: In 480 Epidauros provided eight triremes at Artemision (Hdt. 8.1.2) and ten at Salamis (Hdt. 8.43), as well as 800 men at Plataiai in 479 (Hdt. 9.28.4). Assuming that there is no overlap between the forces, Jameson et al. (1994) 556-59 estimates the military strength of Troizen at 2,800 men, and the total population at c. 16,000 minimum and 23,500 maximum.

chrono_text: Remains of a settlement at Halieis can be traced back to the Geometric period. The town flourished in the Archaic and Classical periods (Jameson et al. (1994) 435-37). fortification on the site can be traced back to at least C7. Excavations show that the town was abandoned in C3e at the latest (Jameson et al. (1994) 436-37).
pop_text: The walls enclosed an area of 18 ha, of which some 15 ha were suitable for habitation in C4m. Assuming a population density of 250 per ha, the town may in C4m have had some 3,750 inhabitants (Jameson et al. (1994) 551).
ha_text: The walls enclosed an area of 18 ha, of which some 15 ha were suitable for habitation in C4m.
relocation_details: In c. 460 Halieis must have been ceded by Hermion to the Tirynthians after they had been expelled from Tiryns by the Argives. They formed a community called Halieis hoi ek Tirynthos.

pop_text: Jameson et al. (1994) 556-59 estimate the military strength of Hermion at 900 men, and the total population at c. 5,000 minimum and 7,500 maximum. Assuming a population density of 250 per ha, the town may in C4m have had some 4,200 inhabitants.
ha_text: The walls enclosed an area of 22.5 ha, of which some 8.5 ha were available for habitation in C6, and almost 17 ha in C4.
relocation_details: In the Archaic and Classical periods it was situated at the tip of a headland between two well-protected natural harbours, but later, perhaps in C2 or C1, it was moved c. 1 km westwards to the far end of the peninsula and to the lower slopes of Mt. Pron.

chrono_text: they had probably been organised by Kleonai from their foundation in 573 ... But at some point in C4l Kleonai was incorporated into Argos and became a civic subdivision of that polis (SEG 30 355 (C4l); IG iv 616 (c. 315)).
pop_text: Its territory covered some 135 km², and its total population has been assessed at c. 8,000 persons (Sakellariou and Faraklas (1971) 122, 125, 128).

chrono_text: The number of sites reached its peak in C5l, and there were considerably fewer sites in C4. In the Heltic period Methana was controlled by the Ptolemaic kings and renamed Arsinoe en Peloponnesoi.
ha_text: The town was on the west coast of the peninsula; it covered an area of c. 5 ha in the Archaic and c. 8 ha in the Classical period.

chrono_text: In c. 460, taking advantage of the weakness of Sparta after the earthquake in 464, the Argives and their allies besieged the Mykenaians... Mykenai was starved into surrender and exposed to an andrapodismos... The city itself was demolished and its territory divided among Argos, Kleonai and Tenea.
pop_text: It provided 80 men at Thermopylai in 480 (Hdt. 7. 202) and, together with Tiryns (no. 356), 400 men at Plataiai in 479 (Hdt. 9. 28. 4).

chrono_text: The only source in which Orneai is called a polis (in the urban sense) is Diod. 16. 34. 3 (r 353/2) and 39. 4 (r 352/1). Paus. 8. 27. 1 (rC5) calls Orneai a polisma, but, referring to his own time, Strabo classifies Orneai as a kome (8. 6. 17).
relocation_details: According to Paus. 2. 25. 6, the inhabitants were transferred to Argos. Pausanias (8. 27. 1) cites the Argive synoecism of Tiryns, Hysiai, Orneai, Mykenai and Mideia as a model for the synoecism of Megalopolis. Subsequently, Orneai was resettled.

pop_text: At Hell. 5.3.16 Xenophon quotes the Lakedaimonians for the statement that Phleious had 5,000 adult male citizens, probably an exaggerated figure (= a total population of 20,000 minimum, with 150 persons per km²!)
ha_text: An urban survey of the town indicates that it covered an area of c. 60 ha (Alcock (1991) 428 fig. 3).
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, bouleuterion, theatre, law_courts, stoa

chrono_text: Polis status in the Archaic and early Classical periods, however, is strongly indicated by the sacred laws of c. 600. In Olympia in 468 a Tirynthian was the winning pugilist in the boys' category. Shortly afterwards Tiryns was taken over by Argive slaves expelled from Argos by the sons of those who fell at Sepeia; after some years of peaceful relations, war broke out between Argos and the former slaves in Tiryns, who eventually were defeated by the Argives. Tiryns was demolished.
pop_text: together with Mykenai (no. 353), it provided 400 men at Plataiai in 479 (Hdt. 9.28.4)

Troizen provided five triremes at Salamis in 480 (Hdt. 8. 43), and 1,000 men at Plataiai in 479 (Hdt. 9. 28. 4). Assuming that there is no overlap between the two forces, Jameson et al. (1994) 556-59 estimate the military strength of Troizen at 2,000 men, and the total population at c. 11,500 minimum and 16,500 maximum.
During the C5e acme, the total population was 35,000-45,000, with 7,000-10,000 slaves/freedmen and 1,500-2,500 males of or above hoplite rank (Figueira (1981) 22-52).
ha_text: The walls enclosed an area of c. 52 ha (Figueira (1981) 39-40.
relocation_details: Heltic inscriptions mention a pentapolis that might indicate a synoecism (Felten (1975) 42-52). In 431, Aiginetans were expelled and scattered. In 405, Lysander refounded Aigina (Xen. Hell. 2.2.9).

chrono_text: From possibly C6l (Hdt. 3.59.1) and during C5-C4l Kalaureia was a possession of Troizen. The sanctuary of Poseidon Kalauros, known from C8e with a temple from C6l, expanded in C5l-C4. After the Lamian War, the polis is presented as the political authority for a grant of ateleia to Siphnos in IG iv 839 = Syll.³ 359 (C4l).

pop_text: There were altogether some 50,000-60,000 adult male citizens (Rhodes (1988) 271-77; Hansen (1988) 23-25). Due to the plague in 430-426, war casualties during the Peloponnesian War, and the siege of 405/4, the number of citizens dropped to some 25,000-30,000 in c. 400 (Hansen (1988) 14-28). During C4 the number of Athenian citizens living in Attika must have been in the range of 30,000, ± a few thousand (Hansen (1985) 65-69; (1994)). The result of a census conducted by Demetrios of Phaleron during the decade 317-307 was 21,000 Athenians and 10,000 metics (Ath. 272 C).
ha_text: The walls of Athens and Peiraieus enclosed altogether 600 ha (Athens: 211 ha, Peiraieus: 300 ha; the space between the Long Walls: 100 ha).
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, bouleuterion, prytaneion, ekklesiastierion, theatre, gymnasium, law_courts, stoa

chrono_text: in the years 403-401 it was a self-governing polity settled with the core of the oligarchic faction in the civil war 404/3. In 401/0 the Athenians marched out in full force and laid siege to Eleusis. the Athenians killed the Eleusinian strategoi and persuaded the Eleusinians to give up Eleusis and move back to Athens, where a new amnesty was declared
relocation_details: The new polis was created by a synoecism of oligarchs from both communities (Arist. Ath. Pol. 39.3: synoikein). In connection with the restoration of the democracy in 403, those of the oligarchs from Athens who so wished could leave Athens and have Eleusis as their residence alongside those of the Eleusinians whom they wanted to retain.

chrono_text: Athenai Diades was a member of the Delian League and may have been among the original members. It is recorded in the tribute lists from 452/1 to 416/15. Athenai Diades must have recovered its independence in C4e since it is recorded among the original members of the Second Athenian Naval League. The inference is that by C4m Athenai must have been incorporated into Histiaia/Oreos, and in C3m it is duly attested as a Histiaian deme.

ha_text: The acropolis and part of the city as well were protected by a C4e circuit wall of c. 2 km enclosing an area of c. 12.5 ha.

chrono_text: The Diakrioi en Euboia were members of the Delian League. They are recorded twice in the tribute lists, in 429/8 and in 417/16.

The Diakres are recorded in the tribute lists from 434/3 (IG i³ 278.vi.25-26) to 416/15 (IG i³ 289.i.15-16), a total of six times. formed perhaps a shortlived splinter community of Chalkis (no. 365) settled in the Diakria in the northern part of the territory of Chalkis in the years after 446 when Chalkis was controlled by Athens

Dion was a member of the Delian League, and may have been among the original members. It is recorded in the tribute lists from 451/50 to 432/1. Dion must have recovered its independence in C4e, since it is recorded among the original members of the Second Athenian Naval League. Dion may have left the League together with Thebes in 371 or shortly afterwards. The presumption is that Dion was a polis in the political sense down to C4f. The inference is that by C4m Dion had been incorporated into Histiaia/Oreos.

Dystos is attested as a settlement in the prehistoric period (Sackett et al. (1966) 76 no. 84) and was refounded in the Archaic period (Hoepfner (1999) 352). Dystos may have been a Dryopian settlement. Like Grynchai (no. 371) and Styra (no. 377), Dystos had once been a polis, but was transformed into an Eretrian deme in, probably, C5f
ha_text: The circuit encloses an area of c. 5 ha.

chrono_text: The first traces of occupation on the acropolis date to the second millennium. Middle Helladic remains have been found in the plain, not far from the future agora. During the Protogeometric period (C9), only the tombs attest to some sort of human presence. The first evidence of settlement, dating from C8, comes from three areas: near the port, on the site of the later sanctuary of Apollo, and at several places at the foot of the acropolis.
pop_text: In C4l Eretria had some 6,000 adult male citizens, as can be inferred retrospectively from a cluster of lists of names of c. 280. A year class of 150-200 ephebes corresponds to a force of some 4,200-5,000 citizens of military age (18-59) and fit for military service, and to a total adult male citizen population of c. 5,750-6,850 minimum, including those over 60 and those unfit for military service.
ha_text: It was about 4,260 m in length; the total area enclosed by the wall was about 81.5 ha.
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, theatre, gymnasium, stoa

chrono_text: Grynchai was a member of the Delian League, and may have been among the original members (ATL iii. 198-99). It belonged to the Island district and is recorded in the tribute lists from 451/50 to 416/5. In C5l, Grynchai became a deme of Eretria.

pop_text: The Athenians are described as colonists (apoikoi, Thuc. 7.57.2; Figueira (1991) 223) and numbered 2,000 (Theopomp. fr. 387) rather than the 1,000 reported at Diod. 12.22.2
relocation_details: After 446 the urban centre was moved to Oreos at modern Kastro, and the inhabitants of the two settlements were merged by a synoikismos (Strabo 10.1.4). Strabo reports that Philistides during his short reign had the population of Ellopia transferred to Oreos (10.1.3).

chrono_text: Coins of C6m (infra) and a Panhellenic victor of C6s (infra) are the only evidence we have for Karystos before the Persian War. Karystos struck coins of silver c. 550-445 and of silver and bronze c. 411-336.
relocation_details: In the Archaic period the urban centre was situated on the Plakari hill, and it may have remained there during C5. In C4, if not before, it was moved to Palaiochori.

chrono_text: Orobiai is described as a polis which was partly engulfed by the great seismic sea wave of 426. Orobiai may in C5 have been a dependent polis lying inside the territory of Histiaia/Oreos but transformed into a Histiaian deme in the course of C4 or C3.
it seems reasonable to infer that Peraia too was once a polis which had been absorbed by Eretria c. 400
Posideion was assessed for phoros in 425/4. The change from polis to deme of Histiaia/Oreos may have taken place in C4f.

During the Lamian War (323/2) Styra was conquered and destroyed by the Athenians (Strabo 10.1.6). In the Heltic period Styra is attested as a deme of Eretria. The presumption is that the change of status from polis to deme took place in C5l or C4e.
the polygonal circuit wall has not been dated, but may belong to C6; Archaic and Classical sherds as well as tiles attest to activity
ha_text: it enclosed an area measuring c. 165 × 70 m

relocation_details: the city was possibly relocated uphill to a new site after the earthquake of 426 (Pritchett (1982) 164; but see Buckler (1989) 93-94); Pritchett (1982) 164-66 describes the probable new site as a walled city.

chrono_text: The earlier fortification was built c. 600 or slightly earlier (Goldman (1940) 430; cf. Fossey (1990) 40) at the 'foundation' of the site.
ha_text: The surface survey carried out by the Cornell Halai East Lokris Project team revealed traces of a C4 or Heltic circuit wall surrounding a lower town and enclosing an area of c. 12-13 ha (Coleman (1992) 268 with fig. 2).

chrono_text: Hope Simpson and Lazenby (1970) 47 reported Archaic and Classical sherds from the Pyrgos area. Late sources such as Strabo 9.4.2 and Paus. 10.1.2 describe Kynos as merely the harbour of Opous (no. 386), but Oldfather (1925) 32 believes that Kynos was originally more than that.
Strabo 14.2.10 describes the relocation of the inhabitants of the three old major poleis in 408/7 to the city of Rhodos as synoecism.
pop_text: Diodorus Siculus 13.75.1 (r 408/7) states that the inhabitants of the three old major poleis relocated to the city of Rhodos, and by the time of the democratic revolution in 395, the city had harbors capable of sheltering large fleets and an agora, indicating a growing population.
ha_text: The urban area enclosed by the city wall in the 3rd century BC was about 430 ha.
relocation_details: In 408/7, the inhabitants of the three old major poleis relocated to the city of Rhodos, a process which Strabo describes as synoecism.
monuments: akropolis, agora, temple, ekklesiastierion, theatre, gymnasium

region: The South Coast of Asia Minor (Pamphylia, Kilikia)
chrono_text: According to Hellan. fr. 15, Aspendos was founded by the hero Aspendos. Other versions of the foundation myth are found in later sources: e.g. that it was an Argive foundation (Strabo 14.4.2).

region: The South Coast of Asia Minor (Pamphylia, Kilikia)
chrono_text: There is no cogent reason to accept Meineke's conjectures 'Ιδυρευς and 'Ιδυρις for MSS Γαυρευς and Γαυρις at Arist. De ventis 973 a 6 -7. The only source to record a city-ethnic is Steph. Byz., who suggests 'Ιδυριτης (327.2). If the identification of Ityra with Idyros is accepted, the presumption is that it was a member of the Delian League and a polis in the political sense too: at least, it was assessed for tribute in 425/4 (IG i³ 71.ii.147 (4,000 dr.)).
pop_text: At least, it was assessed for tribute in 425/4 (IG i³ 71.ii.147 (4,000 dr.)).

region: The South Coast of Asia Minor (Pamphylia, Kilikia)
chrono_text: The toponym is \'Πέργη\', \'ηasper\' (Ps.-Skylax 100, MSS \'πελπίπολις\', conj. Salmassius; Arr. Anab. 1.26.1 (r 333); Strabo 14.4.2; IG i³ 71.ii.113 (425/4)). The city-ethnic is \'Περγαῖος\' (Arr. Anab. 1.25.9 (r 333)); possibly Graeco-Pamphylian \'Πρείιος\' on inscriptions (Merkelbach and S \u0308 ahin (1988) no. 1 (C5l/C4e)).
pop_text: Perge may have been one of the cities allegedly founded by a mixed contingent of people, led by Amphilochos, Mopsos and Kalchas, in the aftermath of the fall of Troy, but the city is not specifically mentioned in the sources (cf. Hdt. 7.91; Strabo 14.4.3).
ha_text: The size of the Roman settlement area was c. 61 ha (Jameson (1974) 375), but the original Greek settlement probably comprised only the northern part of the site, the so-called Acropolis Hill.

region: The South Coast of Asia Minor (Pamphylia, Kilikia)
chrono_text: The city was supposedly colonised from Kyme (no. 817 ) (Ps.Skylax 101 ; Arr. Anab . 1 . 26 . 4 ; Strabo 14 . 4 . 2 ), possibly in C7l (Martin ( 2001 ) 517 ).
ha_text: The city encompassed the whole promontory, c. 45 ha, in Heltic and Roman times, but probably comprised only the outermost peninsula in the Archaic and Classical periods.

region: The South Coast of Asia Minor (Pamphylia, Kilikia)
chrono_text: The territory of Aphrodisias must have comprised the coastal lowland of the river Melas, later, with the foundation of Seleukeia, probably annexed by this city.
pop_text: From c. 520: Aphrodisias struck silver coins on the Aiginetan standard. From c. 485: obv. winged female figure in Knielauf holding kerykeion or staff or wreath; rev. baetyl in incuse square; legend: letters of uncertain meaning.
ha_text: The settlement area on the small plateau above the eastern harbour comprised c. 15 ha, but no extant remains are Greek (TIB 5: 194).

region: The South Coast of Asia Minor (Pamphylia, Kilikia)
chrono_text: The population of Holmoi was transferred to Seleukeia on the Kalykadnos when that city was founded in 312 (Strabo. 14 . 5 . 4 ).

region: The South Coast of Asia Minor (Pamphylia, Kilikia)
chrono_text: Issos was situated on a c. 6, 000 m²-wide coastal plateau near the estuary of the river Pinaros, on the western border of Kilikia Pedia (cf. Diod. 14.21.1; Strabo 14.5.1; for the identification of the site with Yeşil (Kinet) Hüyük, see Hellenkemper (1984)).
pop_text: A silver coinage struck by local officials on the Persian standard from c. 400 has been attributed to Issos (the most recent survey is Brindley (1993)).
